teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord (bhakti) is the highest path, leading to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates bhakti as the culmination of kriya practice, where devotion arises spontaneously from inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Material desires bind one to samsara; offering desires to the Lord purifies them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that desires should be sublimated through kriya, transforming them into devotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's name, even heard once, can liberate even a fallen person.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights the power of nama-japa (chanting) as a key practice in Kriya-Yoga for purification.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna instructs Citraketu on the nature of the self, the illusion of material existence, and the path to liberation through knowledge and devotion. He explains that the soul is distinct from the body and the three states of consciousness, and that by realizing the oneness of the self with the Supreme, one attains freedom.

story.title: Krishna's instruction to Citraketu on the self and liberation
story.arc: Citraketu, having been taught by Narada and Angira, receives direct instruction from Krishna, who reveals the nature of the soul and the path to freedom.
story.timeline_note: After Citraketu's previous teachings from Narada and Angira, Krishna appears and gives final instruction.
story.comic_potential: A dramatic scene where Krishna, as the universal teacher, imparts profound wisdom to a receptive king, with cosmic imagery of the self pervading all.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is distinct from the body and the three states of consciousness (waking, dreaming, deep sleep). By realizing the witness of these states, one attains the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of witnessing the modifications of the mind (vrittis) and abiding in the seer (drashta), leading to samadhi.

teachings.teaching: The material world is a product of maya; the soul is covered by ignorance, leading to repeated birth and death. Human birth is an opportunity for knowledge and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga aims to break the cycle of karma by stilling the mind and realizing the true self beyond maya.

teachings.teaching: One should give up desires and actions based on the pursuit of pleasure and avoidance of pain, as they lead to bondage. Instead, cultivate devotion and knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of vairagya (dispassion) and abhyasa (practice), where one renounces selfish motives and focuses on the divine.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate goal is to see the self in all and all in the self, realizing the oneness of the individual soul with the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga culminates in the realization of unity (samadhi), where the practitioner experiences the identity of atman and Brahman.

chapter_summary: Citraketu, a Vidyadhara king, insults Lord Shiva by mocking his public display of affection with Parvati. Parvati curses him to become a demon. Citraketu accepts the curse with equanimity, teaching about the illusory nature of duality. Lord Shiva then explains to Parvati the greatness of Vishnu's devotees, who are beyond dualities. Citraketu is born as Vritrasura, a demon with divine knowledge.

story.title: The Curse of Citraketu and the Birth of Vritrasura
story.arc: Citraketu's fall and rise as a demon, illustrating the power of devotion and the illusory nature of duality.
story.timeline_note: After Citraketu's previous life as a Vidyadhara king, he is cursed and later born as Vritrasura.
story.comic_potential: The scene of Citraketu mocking Shiva's public affection, followed by Parvati's angry curse, has dramatic and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The wise see all dualities (pleasure/pain, curse/blessing) as illusory, arising from ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of samadhi where the yogi transcends dualities and recognizes the unity of all existence.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial, equally present in all beings; thus a devotee has no enemies.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga leads to this realization of universal love and equality.

teachings.teaching: Devotees of Vishnu are fearless and see no difference between heaven, liberation, and hell.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of inner freedom from all external conditions.

teachings.teaching: One should accept both good and bad fortune as the Lord's will, without attachment.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's practice of surrender (Ishvara pranidhana) is exemplified by Citraketu's acceptance of the curse.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Canto 6, Chapter 18) primarily lists the progeny of various deities and sages, including the descendants of Savitri, Bhaga, Varuna, Mitra, Indra, and especially the lineage of Diti and her sons the Daityas. It also narrates the story of Diti's vow to obtain a son who would kill Indra, and the instructions given by Sage Kashyapa for the vow.

story.title: The Lineage of the Daityas and Diti's Vow
story.arc: Genealogical account and the story of Diti's vow to obtain a son to kill Indra
story.timeline_note: The chapter begins with a genealogical listing of various beings, then focuses on the lineage of Diti and the story of her vow, which occurs after the death of her sons Hiraṇyakaśipu and Hiraṇyākṣa.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic tension of Diti's grief and her secret vow to bear a son who would kill Indra, combined with Kaśyapa's reluctant agreement and the strict rules of the vow, offers rich material for a dramatic or moral narrative.

teachings.teaching: The husband is the supreme deity for a woman; serving him is equivalent to serving the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the practice of devotion through service to one's spouse as a form of karma yoga, where the external relationship mirrors the internal union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Women's nature is fickle and can lead men astray; one should be cautious.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as a warning against attachment to sense objects, which distract from the path of yoga. The 'fickleness' symbolizes the transient nature of material pleasures.

teachings.teaching: One should not harm living beings, curse, lie, cut nails or hair, touch impure things, bathe in water, become angry, or associate with evil persons during a vow.
teachings.lens_note: These are practical disciplines (niyamas) for purifying the mind and body, essential for successful meditation and kriya practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Diti, who performs a year-long vow (Puṃsavana vrata) to obtain a son to kill Indra. Indra, learning of her intention, serves her diligently seeking a ritual flaw. He finds her asleep at dusk with impure feet, enters her womb, and cuts the fetus into seven pieces, which become seven sons. Each piece is further cut into seven, but they do not die. Indra realizes the power of devotion to the Supreme Lord and asks forgiveness. The sons become the Maruts, deities who accompany Indra.

story.title: The Birth of the Maruts
story.arc: Diti's vow and Indra's intervention
story.timeline_note: After the churning of the ocean, before the Tārakāmaya war.
story.comic_potential: Indra's sneaky attempt to find a flaw in Diti's vow, his entry into her womb, and the comical cutting of the fetus into pieces that refuse to die.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Supreme Lord (Hari) yields extraordinary results; even a flawed vow can produce powerful beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that sincere practice (kriya) with devotion (bhakti) can transform even mistakes into spiritual progress. The Maruts' survival despite Indra's attack shows the invincibility of divine grace.

teachings.teaching: One should not seek selfish boons from the Lord; true devotees desire only the Lord Himself.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the highest yoga is desireless action. Indra's realization that even a year of worship yields only a secondary result (siddhi) points to the futility of material goals.

teachings.teaching: Ritual purity and mindfulness are important; Diti's lapse at dusk led to the flaw.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the need for constant awareness (sākṣī-bhāva) in practice. A momentary loss of mindfulness can disrupt even a long-term vow.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Puṃsavana vow (vrata) dedicated to Lord Viṣṇu and Goddess Śrī, including its procedure, mantras, offerings, and the benefits for both men and women who observe it. The vow is performed over twelve months, culminating in a final ceremony. It emphasizes devotion, purity, and the grace of Viṣṇu and Lakṣmī for fulfilling desires.

story.title: The Puṃsavana Vow for Viṣṇu and Lakṣmī
story.arc: Instruction on a specific vrata (vow) to obtain blessings from Viṣṇu and Lakṣmī for various desires, including progeny, wealth, and auspiciousness.
story.timeline_note: The vow is performed over twelve months, starting in the bright fortnight of Mārgaśīrṣa (November-December), with a concluding ceremony on the last day of Kārtika (October-November).
story.comic_potential: The detailed ritual instructions and the promise of fulfilling all desires could be depicted as a step-by-step guide with humorous exaggerations of the benefits.

teachings.teaching: The vow should be undertaken with the husband's permission and involves worship of Viṣṇu and Lakṣmī with specific mantras and offerings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of householder life and the integration of spiritual practice with family duties, where the wife's vow is supported by the husband, reflecting the balance of puruṣa and prakṛti.

teachings.teaching: Viṣṇu is the supreme Puruṣa, the controller of Prakṛti (Māyāśakti), and together with Lakṣmī, they are the cause of the universe.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the union of consciousness (Puruṣa) and energy (Prakṛti) in Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner realizes the divine couple within as the source of all creation.

teachings.teaching: The vow includes daily worship, offerings, and a final homa (fire sacrifice) with twelve oblations.
teachings.lens_note: The twelve oblations correspond to the twelve months of practice, symbolizing the purification of the twelve aspects of the mind and senses through Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The benefits of the vow include obtaining a good husband, children, wealth, beauty, and liberation from sins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as the fruits of Kriya-Yoga: inner stability (husband), spiritual progeny (children), divine qualities (wealth), and purity (beauty).

teachings.teaching: Reciting this chapter during auspicious occasions brings satisfaction to the ancestors and deities, granting all desires.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle that sound vibration (mantra) and intention (sankalpa) harmonize the practitioner with cosmic forces.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Book 7, Chapter 1) addresses the paradox of how enemies of the Lord, such as Śiśupāla and Dantavakra, attain liberation despite their hostility. Through a dialogue between King Yudhiṣṭhira and Nārada, it explains that even hatred, when focused on Kṛṣṇa, can lead to salvation due to constant remembrance. The chapter recounts the story of Jaya and Vijaya, Viṣṇu's gatekeepers, who were cursed to be born as demons, and their subsequent births as Hiraṇyakaśipu, Hiraṇyākṣa, Rāvaṇa, Kumbhakarṇa, Śiśupāla, and Dantavakra, all of whom were killed by the Lord's avatars, thus fulfilling the curse and returning to Vaikuṇṭha.

story.title: The Liberation of the Lord's Enemies
story.arc: Yudhiṣṭhira questions Nārada about how Śiśupāla and Dantavakra attained liberation despite enmity; Nārada explains the power of constant remembrance, even in hatred, and narrates the story of Jaya and Vijaya's curse and subsequent births.
story.timeline_note: The dialogue occurs after the Rājasūya sacrifice of Yudhiṣṭhira, where Śiśupāla was killed. The story of Jaya and Vijaya spans multiple yugas.
story.comic_potential: The irony of enemies attaining liberation through hatred, and the dramatic curse of the gatekeepers, offer rich narrative potential.

teachings.teaching: Even hatred focused on the Lord can lead to liberation due to constant remembrance.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that any intense mental engagement with the divine, regardless of emotional quality, purifies the mind and leads to union.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and beyond the modes of nature; His actions are for the welfare of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Lord's apparent partiality is a teaching tool; in Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner learns to see the same consciousness in all.

teachings.teaching: The mind should be fixed on Kṛṣṇa by any means, as constant absorption leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on steady mental focus (dhyāna) as the key to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The story of Jaya and Vijaya shows that even a curse from sages can be a blessing in disguise, leading to the Lord's association.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the divine play (līlā) where obstacles are opportunities for spiritual growth.

chapter_summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu, enraged by the death of his brother Hiraṇyākṣa at the hands of Viṣṇu (as the boar incarnation), orders the demons to attack the earth and destroy all righteous activities. He then consoles his family with a philosophical discourse on the nature of the self, using the story of King Suyajña and Yama's teachings to illustrate the futility of grief over the body.

story.title: Hiraṇyakaśipu's Grief and Consolation
story.arc: Hiraṇyakaśipu, enraged by his brother's death, orders demons to destroy the earth. He then consoles his family with a discourse on the nature of the self, illustrated by the story of King Suyajña and Yama's teachings.
story.timeline_note: After Hiraṇyākṣa's death by Viṣṇu as the boar.
story.comic_potential: Hiraṇyakaśipu's dramatic rage and philosophical speech, the demon army's destruction, and the embedded story of Yama as a child teaching queens.

teachings.teaching: The self (ātman) is eternal, pure, and unaffected by the body's changes. Grief over the dead is due to ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the need to transcend identification with the body through disciplined practice, recognizing the self as the witness.

teachings.teaching: The body is like a house made of elements, subject to time and change. The self is distinct and unattached.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view of the body as a temporary instrument for spiritual evolution, not the true self.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve for what is inevitable; the wise understand the nature of reality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework encourages acceptance of life's transience as a step toward inner peace and focused practice.

chapter_summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu teaches his sister-in-law Diti and the other widows about the nature of the self and the futility of grief, using the story of a male bird (kuliṅga) who is shot while lamenting his mate's death. He argues that attachment to the body leads to suffering, and that one should not mourn for the body or the self, as the true self is eternal. Diti, upon hearing this, gives up her grief and focuses on spiritual truth.

story.title: Hiraṇyakaśipu's Discourse on the Futility of Grief
story.arc: Hiraṇyakaśipu consoles Diti and the widows after the death of Suyajña, using the parable of the birds to illustrate the nature of the self and the transience of the body.
story.timeline_note: After the death of Suyajña, during the mourning period.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic irony of a demon teaching detachment, and the sudden death of the male bird mid-lament.

teachings.teaching: One should not mourn for the body or the self, as the true self is eternal and beyond grief.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching corresponds to the practice of disidentification from the body and mind, recognizing the witness consciousness (sākṣī) that is unaffected by events.

teachings.teaching: Attachment to the body leads to suffering; understanding the distinction between self and other liberates one from grief.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes viveka (discrimination) between the eternal self and the temporary body, which is the foundation of Kriya-Yoga practice.

chapter_summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu performs severe penance to become immortal and invincible. The gods, distressed by his tapas, appeal to Brahmā. Brahmā visits Hiraṇyakaśipu, restores his body, and offers a boon. Hiraṇyakaśipu requests immunity from death by any being, weapon, time, place, and other conditions, along with universal sovereignty.

story.title: Hiraṇyakaśipu's Penance and Boon
story.arc: Hiraṇyakaśipu performs extreme tapas to gain immortality and invincibility. The gods seek Brahmā's intervention. Brahmā appears and offers a boon. Hiraṇyakaśipu requests specific protections against death and universal dominion.
story.timeline_note: Set in the Satya Yuga, after Hiraṇyakaśipu's brother Hiraṇyākṣa was killed by Varāha.
story.comic_potential: Hiraṇyakaśipu's body being eaten by ants while he remains in meditation, then restored by Brahmā's water, has vivid visual and absurdist humor.

teachings.teaching: The power of severe penance (tapas) can compel even the gods to grant boons.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, tapas is the disciplined practice of inner fire, burning karma and purifying the mind. Hiraṇyakaśipu's external austerity mirrors the internal tapas of yogic practice.

teachings.teaching: Brahmā is the creator and the inner controller of all beings, manifesting as time, life, and consciousness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees Brahmā as the cosmic intelligence (Mahat) that governs the play of guṇas. Hiraṇyakaśipu's hymn acknowledges this universal principle.

teachings.teaching: Desire for immortality and invincibility stems from ego and attachment to the body, which is ultimately impermanent.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes transcending the ego. Hiraṇyakaśipu's boon request reflects the asura's attachment to personal power, a hindrance to spiritual liberation.

chapter_summary: Brahma grants boons to Hiranyakashipu after his severe penance. Empowered, Hiranyakashipu conquers all worlds and usurps Indra's throne, oppressing the universe. The gods seek refuge in Vishnu, who assures them that Hiranyakashipu will be killed when he persecutes his own son Prahlada, a great devotee. Prahlada's exemplary devotion and qualities are described.

story.title: Hiranyakashipu's Boon and Oppression, and Prahlada's Devotion
story.arc: Hiranyakashipu gains power through boons, becomes tyrant; gods seek Vishnu's help; Vishnu promises intervention through Prahlada.
story.timeline_note: After Hiranyakashipu's penance and boon, before his death by Narasimha.
story.comic_potential: Hiranyakashipu's opulent rule and the gods' fear contrasted with Prahlada's ecstatic devotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects those who take refuge in Him; He will destroy the wicked when they persecute His devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle that divine grace (kripa) is activated by sincere surrender and devotion, leading to the dissolution of ego (asura) through the inner 'Narasimha' of awareness.

teachings.teaching: A true devotee (like Prahlada) is detached from material qualities, equipoised, and absorbed in love for God.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that such absorption (tanmayatā) is the fruit of sustained meditation (dhyāna) and surrender, leading to inner stillness and joy.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana describes the demon king Hiranyakashipu's anger towards his son Prahlada for his devotion to Vishnu, and his attempts to kill Prahlada. The chapter includes Hiranyakashipu's orders to his servants to torment Prahlada, and Prahlada's unwavering faith.

story.title: Hiranyakashipu's Anger and Prahlada's Devotion
story.arc: Hiranyakashipu, enraged by Prahlada's devotion to Vishnu, attempts to kill him but fails due to Prahlada's faith.
story.timeline_note: This occurs during the reign of Hiranyakashipu, before the advent of Narasimha.
story.comic_potential: High drama with Hiranyakashipu's rage and Prahlada's calm devotion.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to God protects the devotee from all harm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the power of unwavering focus on the divine, which aligns the practitioner with cosmic will.

chapter_summary: Narada asks Lord Brahma why a father would give a sin to his innocent son, and Brahma explains that parents sometimes discipline their children for their own good, but never out of malice. He cites the example of Hiranyakashipu's hatred for his son Prahlada, which led to Prahlada's death.

story.title: The Sin of a Father
story.arc: Narada questions Brahma about Hiranyakashipu's hatred for Prahlada
story.timeline_note: During the reign of Hiranyakashipu
story.comic_potential: Dramatic dialogue between sage and creator god

teachings.teaching: Parents may discipline children for their education, not out of anger.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of disciplined action (kriya) without attachment to results, where even harsh actions can be for the ultimate good.

chapter_summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu's son Prahlāda, despite being taught by demonic gurus, develops unwavering devotion to Viṣṇu. He teaches that the highest duty is bhakti (nine-fold devotion) to Viṣṇu. Hiraṇyakaśipu becomes enraged, attempts to kill Prahlāda through various means, but all fail due to Prahlāda's divine protection.

story.title: Prahlāda's Unwavering Devotion
story.arc: Prahlāda, born to demon king Hiraṇyakaśipu, becomes a devotee of Viṣṇu despite being taught by demonic gurus. He teaches bhakti to his father, who tries to kill him but fails.
story.timeline_note: This chapter is part of the narrative of the 7th Skandha of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa, narrated by Nārada to Yudhiṣṭhira.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic confrontations between father and son, failed assassination attempts, divine protection, and philosophical debates.

teachings.teaching: The highest good for embodied beings is to abandon the household life (gṛhamandhakūpa) and take shelter of Hari (Viṣṇu).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend worldly attachments and focus on inner spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The distinction between 'self' and 'other' is an illusion created by māyā; the true self is one with the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes non-duality and the dissolution of ego through meditation.

teachings.teaching: The nine limbs of bhakti: śravaṇa, kīrtana, smaraṇa, pādasevana, arcana, vandana, dāsya, sakhya, ātmanivedana.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga incorporates these as practical methods for cultivating devotion and inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: Those who are attached to sense objects (gṛhavratānām) cannot develop devotion to Kṛṣṇa; they are like animals chewing the cud.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses control of senses and turning inward to realize the self.

teachings.teaching: The ignorant do not know that the true goal of life is Viṣṇu; they are led by blind leaders into bondage.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga advocates for a guru who can guide one beyond material illusion.

teachings.teaching: Devotion arises only when one takes shelter of the dust of the feet of great souls (niṣkiñcanānām).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the importance of association with realized beings.

chapter_summary: Hiranyakashipu's son Prahlada is taught by the asura teachers Shanda and Amarka, but rejects their instruction on dharma, artha, and kama. When the teachers leave, Prahlada teaches his playmates about devotion to Vishnu. The teachers report to Hiranyakashipu, who orders Prahlada to be bound and thrown into the sea.

story.title: Prahlada's Rejection of Material Teachings
story.arc: Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, refuses to accept the worldly teachings of his asura teachers and instead instructs his friends in bhakti.
story.timeline_note: After the teachers' initial instruction, Prahlada teaches his playmates; the teachers then report to Hiranyakashipu.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between the serious asura teachers and the innocent, wise child Prahlada could be played for humor.

teachings.teaching: The pursuit of dharma, artha, and kama is not the ultimate goal; devotion to the Supreme is higher.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the need to transcend the three aims of life and focus on inner realization through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: True knowledge comes from within and is not dependent on external teachers who are attached to duality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga awakens innate wisdom, not merely intellectual learning from others.

chapter_summary: Prahlada instructs the Daitya children on the urgency of cultivating devotion to Vishnu from childhood, critiquing worldly attachments and emphasizing the supreme goal of surrendering to the Lord. He recounts that this knowledge was taught by Narada, who received it from Narayana-Nara. The Daitya children then ask Prahlada how he, as a child confined to the palace, gained association with great souls.

story.title: Prahlada's Discourse on Bhāgavata Dharma
story.arc: Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, instructs the young Daityas on the importance of starting spiritual life early and renouncing worldly attachments. He reveals the source of his knowledge as Narada, who learned from Narayana-Nara. The Daitya children then question how he gained such wisdom despite his confined upbringing.
story.timeline_note: Set during Prahlada's childhood, before his father Hiranyakashipu's persecution.
story.comic_potential: A young Prahlada, surrounded by demon children, calmly explaining the futility of sense pleasures while they listen with curiosity.

teachings.teaching: One should cultivate devotion to Vishnu from childhood, as human life is rare and fleeting.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the urgency of beginning spiritual practice (sādhana) early, before the body decays and the mind becomes entangled.

teachings.teaching: Sense pleasures are available everywhere and are not worth pursuing at the cost of spiritual life.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would note that Kriya-Yoga redirects the senses inward, recognizing that external pleasures are transient and do not yield lasting peace.

teachings.teaching: Human life is wasted in sleep, childhood play, old age, and household attachments; only a small fraction is available for spiritual growth.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on using every moment for practice, as time is limited and the body is impermanent.

teachings.teaching: Attachment to family and wealth is a strong bondage that is difficult to break.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches detachment (vairāgya) as essential; one must see the self as distinct from family roles.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon association with materialistic demons and take shelter of Narayana, the Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this means turning away from sense objects and fixing the mind on the inner Self, which is identical with the Lord.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Acyuta (Vishnu) is easy because He is the Self of all beings and already attained.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the realization that the Lord is one's own true nature; Kriya-Yoga aims to directly experience this unity.

teachings.teaching: The highest goal is to please Adhokṣaja (Vishnu) through compassion and friendship towards all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes selfless service and seeing the Lord in all, which purifies the heart.

teachings.teaching: The three aims of life (dharma, artha, kāma) are fulfilled by surrendering to the Supreme Person.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would say that Kriya-Yoga integrates all aspects of life into devotion, making even worldly activities spiritual.

teachings.teaching: This pure knowledge was taught by Narayana to Narada, and then to Prahlada; it is attainable only by the humble.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of guru-paramparā (teacher succession) in Kriya-Yoga; knowledge is transmitted through a lineage.

chapter_summary: Prahlada recounts his early life and the teachings he received from Narada while in the womb. He describes the battle between devas and asuras, his mother's capture by Indra, her rescue by Narada, and the spiritual instruction she received. Prahlada then delivers a discourse on the nature of the self, the path of bhakti, and the futility of material pursuits, urging the asura children to surrender to Hari.

story.title: Prahlada's Discourse on Bhakti
story.arc: Prahlada, questioned by the asura children, narrates his early life and the teachings he received, culminating in a philosophical discourse on the nature of the self and the path of devotion to Hari.
story.timeline_note: Recounted by Prahlada after his father Hiranyakashipu's departure for penance; events occur during Prahlada's gestation and early childhood.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes, capture and rescue of a queen, a sage's intervention, and a child sage delivering profound teachings to demon children.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is eternal, unchanging, pure, and distinct from the body and its transformations.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the purusha as distinct from prakriti, achieved through sustained meditative practice.

teachings.teaching: The path to liberation is bhakti (devotion) to Hari, which includes hearing, chanting, remembering, serving, worshiping, and surrendering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that bhakti is the effortless yoga that purifies the mind and leads to direct experience of the divine, bypassing complex rituals.

teachings.teaching: Material possessions, relationships, and achievements are transient and cannot provide lasting happiness; only devotion to Hari yields eternal bliss.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's critique of worldly pursuits as sources of bondage; Kriya-Yoga redirects energy inward to the eternal self.

teachings.teaching: The cycle of karma and rebirth is rooted in ignorance; by realizing the self and cultivating devotion, one attains liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views karma as a seed that can be burned through the fire of knowledge and devotion, leading to cessation of rebirth.

teachings.teaching: One should see Hari in all beings and respect them accordingly, practicing non-enmity and compassion.
teachings.lens_note: This universal compassion is a natural outcome of Kriya-Yoga practice, where the practitioner perceives the same consciousness in all.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana emphasizes that pure devotion (bhakti) to Lord Hari is the sole means to please Him, surpassing all other practices like charity, austerity, sacrifice, cleanliness, or vows. It declares that even beings considered lowly—such as demons, yakshas, rakshasas, women, shudras, cowherds, birds, animals, and sinful creatures—have attained union with the Lord through unwavering devotion. The ultimate purpose of human life is single-minded devotion to Govinda, seeing everything as His manifestation.

story.title: The Power of Pure Devotion
story.arc: Teaching on bhakti as the sole means to please the Lord
story.timeline_note: Part of a discourse by Prahlada to the demons, likely during the time of Hiranyakashipu.
story.comic_potential: A dramatic contrast between the futility of external practices and the transformative power of devotion, with examples of even demons attaining the Lord.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is pleased not by virtuous deeds, knowledge, charity, austerity, sacrifice, cleanliness, or vows, but by untainted devotion alone; all else is mere pretense.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that inner devotion (bhakti) is the core of yoga, not external rituals. The 'untainted devotion' corresponds to the pure intention cultivated through kriya practices, where the mind is fixed on the divine without distraction.

teachings.teaching: Therefore, O demons, cultivate devotion to Lord Hari, the Supreme Being, seeing all beings and the Lord as equal to your own self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets 'seeing all as equal to one's self' as the realization of the universal Self (Atman) through meditation, leading to compassion and unity consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Even demons, yakshas, rakshasas, women, shudras, cowherds, birds, animals, and sinful beings have attained union with the Lord through devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the Kriya-Yoga principle that devotion transcends all social and karmic barriers; anyone can attain liberation through sincere practice, regardless of their background.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate purpose of human life is single-minded devotion to Govinda, seeing everything as His manifestation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's framework, 'single-minded devotion' is the culmination of kriya practice, where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, perceiving the world as the play of the Lord.

chapter_summary: Narada narrates how Hiranyakashipu's son Prahlada becomes a devotee of Vishnu, defying his father. Hiranyakashipu tries to kill Prahlada, but Vishnu appears as Narasimha (half-man, half-lion) from a pillar, kills Hiranyakashipu, and is praised by gods and sages.

story.title: The Appearance of Narasimha and the Death of Hiranyakashipu
story.arc: Prahlada's devotion leads to divine intervention; Vishnu incarnates as Narasimha to protect his devotee and destroy the demon king.
story.timeline_note: This event occurs after Hiranyakashipu's boon from Brahma and during Prahlada's childhood.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic emergence of Narasimha from a pillar, the fierce battle, and the cosmic reactions provide vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the inner self of all beings; one should see the same Lord in all.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the practice of seeing the divine in all beings, which purifies the mind and leads to inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: True strength is not physical but spiritual; the Lord protects his devotees.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle that inner surrender to the divine yields protection and grace, transcending egoic power.

teachings.teaching: The Lord appears in any form to protect the righteous and destroy evil.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary highlights that the divine responds to the sincere call of the devotee, manifesting as needed for the devotee's spiritual evolution.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, various celestial beings (Manus, Prajapatis, Gandharvas, Caranas, Yaksas, Kimpurusas, Vaitālikas, Kinnaras, and Visnuparsadas) praise Lord Narasimha for slaying the demon Hiranyakasipu, who had oppressed them. Each group recounts how the demon had subjugated them and expresses gratitude for their liberation.

story.title: The Celestial Beings Praise Narasimha
story.arc: After Narasimha kills Hiranyakasipu, various groups of celestial beings offer prayers and thanks, recounting their liberation from the demon's tyranny.
story.timeline_note: Immediately following the slaying of Hiranyakasipu by Narasimha.
story.comic_potential: A series of comedic relief as each group of beings, in their own characteristic style, complains about the demon's misdeeds and then praises the Lord.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked, as seen in Narasimha's slaying of Hiranyakasipu.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner battle where the divine will (Narasimha) destroys the ego (Hiranyakasipu) that oppresses the true self (the celestial beings).

teachings.teaching: Devotees find liberation and joy when the Lord removes obstacles (the demon) from their spiritual path.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the removal of karmic impurities through the practice of kriya, leading to inner peace.

chapter_summary: Narada narrates how after Hiranyakashipu's death, the gods, led by Brahma and Shiva, were unable to approach the enraged Lord Narasimha. They sent Lakshmi, who also hesitated, and then Brahma sent Prahlada. Prahlada prostrated at the Lord's feet, and the Lord, pleased, placed his lotus hand on Prahlada's head. Prahlada then offered a profound prayer (stuti) glorifying the Lord, emphasizing the supremacy of devotion over material qualities, the Lord's impartial mercy, and the futility of worldly pursuits. The Lord offered Prahlada a boon, but Prahlada, being an exclusive devotee, refused material boons.

story.title: Prahlada's Prayer and the Lord's Grace
story.arc: After the death of Hiranyakashipu, the gods are unable to pacify the fierce Narasimha. Brahma sends Prahlada, who offers a sublime prayer. The Lord becomes pleased and offers a boon, but Prahlada, being a pure devotee, refuses material boons.
story.timeline_note: Immediately after the Narasimha avatar's killing of Hiranyakashipu.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between the fierce Narasimha and the calm, devoted Prahlada; the gods' fear and hesitation; the dramatic prayer.

teachings.teaching: Devotion (bhakti) is superior to all material qualities like wealth, birth, beauty, austerity, learning, etc. The Lord is pleased only by devotion, as seen in the case of Gajendra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that inner purification through devotion is the essence of yoga, not external practices.

teachings.teaching: A devotee, even if born in a low family, is superior to a learned brahmin who is averse to the Lord's feet. The devotee purifies his whole family.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle that true worth is measured by one's connection to the divine, not by social status.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-sufficient and does not need our worship; whatever honor we give to Him is actually for our own benefit, like a face reflected in a mirror.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's teaching that all actions are ultimately for self-purification, not for God's benefit.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's incarnations are for the protection of the world and the pleasure of His devotees, not out of any need.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga views divine incarnations as manifestations of cosmic order for the upliftment of beings.

teachings.teaching: The true fear is not of the Lord's fierce form but of the cycle of samsara (birth and death). The Lord is the only refuge.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees the goal as liberation from the cycle of karma through devotion and self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The senses pull the mind in different directions like co-wives tormenting a husband. One must control them through devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on sense control as a prerequisite for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's mercy is impartial; He bestows grace according to one's service, like a wish-fulfilling tree.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that grace is proportional to one's effort and surrender.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is both the material and efficient cause of the universe, pervading everything like fire in wood.
teachings.lens_note: This non-dual vision is central to Kriya-Yoga's understanding of the unity of existence.

teachings.teaching: The highest goal is exclusive devotion (ekantita) to the Lord, not any material boon.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga culminates in selfless devotion as the ultimate expression of yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Skandha 7, Chapter 10) narrates the aftermath of Narasimha's killing of Hiranyakashipu. Prahlada refuses boons from the Lord, teaching the nature of pure devotion. The Lord grants that Hiranyakashipu is purified. Brahma praises the Lord. The Lord explains that demons like Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha, though killed, attain liberation through constant enmity, as seen in later births as Ravana, Kumbhakarna, and Shishupala. The chapter concludes with the glories of this narrative.

story.title: Prahlada's Devotion and the Fate of Demons
story.arc: After Narasimha kills Hiranyakashipu, Prahlada refuses boons, teaching pure devotion. The Lord purifies Hiranyakashipu. Brahma praises the Lord. The Lord explains that demons who constantly think of Him in enmity also attain liberation, as seen in later births of Hiranyaksha and Hiranyakashipu as Ravana and Kumbhakarna (killed by Rama), and then as Shishupala (killed by Krishna). The chapter ends with the glories of this narrative.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs immediately after Narasimha's killing of Hiranyakashipu, and includes flash-forwards to future incarnations (Rama, Krishna) and births of demons.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between Prahlada's serene devotion and the violent deaths of demons, along with the cosmic cycle of enmity leading to liberation, offers dramatic and philosophical depth.

teachings.teaching: A true devotee does not seek material boons from the Lord; rather, he desires freedom from desires themselves.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle of 'vairagya' (dispassion) as a prerequisite for inner stillness and union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: When one abandons all desires in the mind, one becomes fit for realizing the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind of desires, leading to the state of 'bhagavattva' (divine nature).

teachings.teaching: Even enemies who constantly think of the Lord with enmity attain liberation, as their minds are fixed on Him.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the power of constant remembrance (smarana), a key Kriya-Yoga practice, regardless of the emotional quality.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and reciting this sacred narrative purifies one and frees from karmic bonds.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the power of 'sravana' and 'kirtana' (hearing and chanting) as integral to Kriya-Yoga sadhana.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana describes the glory of Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, and the defeat of the demon Hiranyakashipu by Narasimha. It emphasizes the power of devotion and the protection of the Lord for his devotees.

story.title: The Glory of Prahlada and Narasimha
story.arc: Devotion and divine protection
story.timeline_note: This is a condensed summary verse from the Bhagavata Purana, likely part of a larger narrative.
story.comic_potential: High - dramatic battle between demon and lion-man, with devotional elements.

teachings.teaching: One who faithfully recites or hears the story of Prahlada and Narasimha attains fearlessness and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the transformative power of sravana (hearing) and kirtana (chanting) as means to purify the mind and realize the self.

chapter_summary: Narada narrates the story of Tripura-daha (the burning of the three cities) to King Yudhishthira. The demons, led by Maya, built three impenetrable cities and harassed the gods. Lord Shiva, empowered by Vishnu's grace, destroyed the cities with a single arrow. The chapter highlights the supremacy of Vishnu and the power of divine intervention.

story.title: The Burning of the Three Cities
story.arc: Destruction of Tripura by Shiva with Vishnu's aid
story.timeline_note: Past event narrated by Narada to Yudhishthira
story.comic_potential: Maya's magical cities, Vishnu and Brahma as cow and calf drinking the nectar, Shiva's single arrow destroying the cities

teachings.teaching: No one—deva, asura, or human—can overcome destiny; all are subject to divine will.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes surrender to the divine plan and the futility of ego-driven action.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes (lila) are meant to purify the world and inspire devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens: Hearing such stories purifies the mind and prepares it for meditation on the inner Self.

chapter_summary: Nārada, at Yudhiṣṭhira's request, expounds the sanātana dharma (eternal duties) for all varṇas and āśramas, as heard from Nārāyaṇa. He details the thirty characteristics of dharma, the duties of brāhmaṇas, kṣatriyas, vaiśyas, śūdras, and women, and emphasizes that one should follow one's own nature while gradually transcending guṇas.

story.title: Nārada's Discourse on Sanātana Dharma
story.arc: Yudhiṣṭhira asks Nārada about the eternal dharma for humans; Nārada replies with a detailed exposition of duties for all varṇas and āśramas.
story.timeline_note: After the description of Prahlāda's deeds in previous chapter.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic discourse.

teachings.teaching: The supreme dharma for all humans is devotion to Nārāyaṇa, consisting of thirty characteristics including truth, compassion, austerity, cleanliness, forbearance, control of mind and senses, non-violence, celibacy, charity, self-study, simplicity, contentment, equal vision, service, withdrawal from worldly pursuits, introspection, silence, self-reflection, sharing food, seeing God in all beings, and nine forms of bhakti.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these thirty characteristics are the practical disciplines (kriyas) that purify the mind and prepare it for meditation, leading to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The duties of brāhmaṇas include study, teaching, sacrifice, officiating, giving and receiving gifts; for kṣatriyas: protecting people, taxation, and not accepting gifts; for vaiśyas: agriculture, trade, and serving brāhmaṇas; for śūdras: serving the twice-born.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these duties are not mere social roles but kriyas that align one's actions with one's inherent nature (svabhāva), which is the starting point of yoga.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa should avoid the dog-like profession (śvavṛtti) of serving the lowly; in times of distress, he may adopt lower occupations but never that of a kṣatriya.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the importance of maintaining purity of means even in adversity, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga where the means are as important as the goal.

teachings.teaching: The characteristics of a brāhmaṇa are calmness, self-control, austerity, cleanliness, contentment, forgiveness, simplicity, knowledge, compassion, and truth; of a kṣatriya: heroism, valor, firmness, brilliance, charity, self-conquest, forgiveness, devotion to brāhmaṇas, and truth; of a vaiśya: devotion to gods and guru, pursuit of dharma-artha-kāma, faith, effort, and skill; of a śūdra: humility, cleanliness, service to the master, and truth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views these guṇas as indicators of one's spiritual progress; they are cultivated through consistent practice of kriyas.

teachings.teaching: A wife should serve her husband as a devotee serves the Lord, with love, truth, and dedication; she should be content, not greedy, skilled, and pure.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the surrender of the individual ego (wife) to the higher Self (husband), a key practice for inner union.

teachings.teaching: One should follow the dharma natural to one's own nature (svabhāva) and gradually transcend the guṇas by giving up the karma born of one's nature, just as a field becomes barren by repeated plowing.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya-Yoga: using one's natural tendencies as a springboard to go beyond them through disciplined action and meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana, narrated by Narada, outlines the duties and disciplines of a brahmacārī (celibate student), vānaprastha (forest-dweller), and yati (renunciant). It details the daily routine, conduct, and spiritual practices for each stage, culminating in the dissolution of the self into the Absolute.

story.title: The Duties of the Four Āśramas
story.arc: Instruction on the progressive stages of life leading to liberation
story.timeline_note: Timeless instruction; no specific historical timeline.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic and procedural.

teachings.teaching: A brahmacārī should reside in the guru's house, serving him with humility and devotion, and perform daily worship of the guru, fire, sun, and deities.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes disciplined service (seva) as a foundation for inner purification and energy regulation.

teachings.teaching: The brahmacārī should beg for alms and offer them to the guru, eating only with permission, and maintain celibacy and control of the senses.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights that begging (bhikṣā) cultivates humility and detachment, while sense control is essential for prāṇāyāma and meditation.

teachings.teaching: One should avoid association with women and even avoid grooming services from young women, as the senses are powerful and can disturb even a renunciant.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as a warning against energy leakage; preservation of vital energy (ojas) is crucial for spiritual ascent.

teachings.teaching: Duality persists as long as one perceives the world as separate from the Self; non-duality is realized when one sees the Lord in all.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the goal of meditation: merging the individual consciousness with the cosmic consciousness through prāṇāyāma and mantra.

teachings.teaching: After completing brahmacarya, one may enter household life, forest life, or renunciation, but all should see the Lord in fire, guru, self, and all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that regardless of āśrama, the practice of seeing unity (sama-darśana) is the essence of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: A vānaprastha should eat only naturally obtained food, perform sacrifices with forest produce, and endure heat, cold, and other hardships.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views this as a training in tapas (austerity) to strengthen the mind and body for higher yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: When the body becomes unfit for duties due to disease or old age, one should perform gradual withdrawal of the senses and elements into their sources, culminating in the realization of the pure consciousness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches this as a systematic prāṇāyāma technique: withdrawing prāṇa from each sense and element, leading to samādhi.

chapter_summary: Narada describes the ideal renunciate (paramahamsa) who, having realized the non-dual Self, wanders alone, detached, and indifferent to worldly concerns. He recounts the story of a sage (the 'ajagara' or python-like sage) who, lying on the bank of the Kaveri, is questioned by Prahlada about his apparent contradictory state of being both inactive and corpulent. The sage explains that he has transcended all desires and actions, having seen the futility of worldly pursuits, and lives like a python, accepting whatever comes. He teaches that true happiness lies in the Self, not in external objects, and that one should give up all attachments and dualities, offering everything into the fire of self-realization.

story.title: The Python Sage and King Prahlada
story.arc: Narada describes the ideal renunciate and then narrates the encounter between Prahlada and a sage who lives like a python, teaching detachment.
story.timeline_note: The story is set in the Treta Yuga or earlier, as Prahlada is a devotee of Narayana from the Satya Yuga.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between the corpulent, inactive sage and his profound teachings could be played for gentle humor, as Prahlada's initial confusion mirrors the reader's.

teachings.teaching: The paramahamsa renunciate should wander alone, detached, seeing the Self in all, and not be attached to any external signs or scriptures.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the stage of 'sannyasa' where the yogi, having realized the Self, abandons all external practices and lives in constant inner awareness.

teachings.teaching: One should not rejoice in life nor fear death, but wait for time as the end of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of equanimity (samatva) towards the pairs of opposites, a key aspect of inner purification.

teachings.teaching: The sage should not engage in false scriptures, debates, or take sides; he should not teach disciples or accumulate books.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the advanced yogi transcends intellectual pursuits and lives in direct experience, beyond words.

teachings.teaching: The sage may appear mad or dumb, but his actions reveal the truth to the wise.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga idea that the realized being's behavior may seem unconventional but is a teaching in itself.

teachings.teaching: Desire (tṛṣṇā) drives one through countless births; seeing the futility of worldly actions, the sage renounces them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga identifies desire as the root of karma and bondage; its dissolution through inner practice leads to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Self's true nature is bliss, but due to ignorance, one seeks happiness externally, leading to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: This is central to Kriya-Yoga: the goal is to realize the inner Self as the source of all happiness, not external objects.

teachings.teaching: The wealthy suffer from constant fear and anxiety; the wise give up desire for life and wealth.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind, removing the fears that arise from attachment.

teachings.teaching: The bee and the python are great teachers: the bee teaches detachment from hoarding, the python teaches contentment with what comes.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these are practical examples of vairāgya (dispassion) and santoṣa (contentment), essential for inner progress.

teachings.teaching: The sage accepts whatever food and clothing come his way, without preference, and sleeps anywhere.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga ideal of 'yadṛcchā' (accepting what comes) as a means to transcend likes and dislikes.

teachings.teaching: One should neither praise nor blame others, but wish for all to attain oneness with the Supreme Self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes universal love and non-judgment as fruits of self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The sage should offer all dualities (vikalpa) into the mind, the mind into the ego, the ego into māyā, and māyā into the realization of the Self, then rest in the Self.
teachings.lens_note: This is a direct description of the Kriya-Yoga process of inner sacrifice (yajña) leading to samādhi, where the yogi merges all distinctions into the Self.

chapter_summary: Narada instructs Yudhishthira on the duties of a householder (grihastha), emphasizing detachment, charity, worship of Krishna through Vedic rituals, and the importance of holy places and times. The chapter underscores that the ultimate recipient of all offerings is the Supreme Lord, and that the brahmana is the best recipient.

story.title: Duties of a Householder
story.arc: Instruction on householder dharma
story.timeline_note: After Yudhishthira's coronation, during his reign.
story.comic_potential: A dialogue between a king and a sage, with lists of holy places and times.

teachings.teaching: A householder should perform his duties as an offering to Vasudeva and associate with great sages.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this means integrating all actions as a form of yoga, dedicating every act to the divine.

teachings.teaching: One should gradually detach from family and possessions by associating with saints, realizing they are like a dream.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of vairagya (detachment) through inner discipline.

teachings.teaching: A wise person should be detached internally but act as if attached externally, using the body and home only as needed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the balance between inner renunciation and outer engagement, a key Kriya-Yoga principle.

teachings.teaching: One should not be overly possessive; only as much as needed to sustain the body is one's own; excess is theft.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching of simplicity and non-accumulation.

teachings.teaching: See all creatures as one's own children, recognizing the equality of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes universal compassion as a foundation for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: One should pursue dharma, artha, and kama without excessive effort, according to time and place.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga advocates moderation and alignment with natural order.

teachings.teaching: Share with others, even if only one's own wife, to overcome possessiveness.
teachings.lens_note: This practice of sharing cultivates detachment, a core Kriya-Yoga value.

teachings.teaching: Renounce attachment to the wife for whom one might sacrifice life or commit sin; by conquering that attachment, one conquers the unconquerable.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as conquering the mind through control of the strongest attachment.

teachings.teaching: The body is temporary, ending in worms, feces, or ashes; attachment to it and to one's wife is foolish compared to the eternal self.
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the Kriya-Yoga focus on the immortal soul over the perishable body.

teachings.teaching: Live on remnants of sacrifices; renounce ownership of surplus to attain the path of the great.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga encourages simplicity and offering the fruits of action.

teachings.teaching: Worship the Supreme Person through offerings to gods, sages, beings, ancestors, and oneself, using honestly earned wealth.
teachings.lens_note: This integrates all life into a continuous worship, a key Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Perform Vedic sacrifices like Agnihotra when one has the means, as the Lord is pleased by offerings made into the fire or into the mouth of a brahmana.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as the external counterpart to internal Kriya-Yoga fire offerings.

teachings.teaching: Worship the Knower of the Field (Krishna) through brahmanas, offering various desired items.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes service to the wise as a form of worship.

teachings.teaching: Perform shraddha for ancestors in the month of Praushthapada (Bhadrapada) and other specified times.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga honors ancestral rites as part of dharma.

teachings.teaching: Auspicious times for spiritual activities include solstices, equinoxes, eclipses, and specific tithis.
teachings.lens_note: Aligning practice with cosmic rhythms enhances Kriya-Yoga effectiveness.

teachings.teaching: Bathing, chanting, sacrifice, vows, and worship done at these times are imperishable.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the potency of sacred times for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The best holy place is where a worthy recipient (satpātra) is found.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that the true pilgrimage is to find and serve a realized soul.

teachings.teaching: The universe is the form of the Lord; holy places are where His image is worshipped and where brahmanas with tapas and knowledge reside.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga sees the entire world as sacred, but certain places amplify spiritual energy.

teachings.teaching: Places like Kurukshetra, Gaya, Prayaga, etc., are supremely holy; visiting them yields great merit.
teachings.lens_note: These are recommended for Kriya-Yoga practitioners to enhance their sadhana.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate recipient of all offerings is Hari alone, as He pervades all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that all worship ultimately reaches the Supreme, unifying all paths.

teachings.teaching: Among gods, sages, and brahmanas, Acyuta (Krishna) is considered the best recipient for first offerings.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga prioritizes devotion to the personal form of God.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Acyuta satisfies all living beings, as He is the root of the cosmic tree.
teachings.lens_note: This is a core Kriya-Yoga teaching: serving the whole through the one.

teachings.teaching: The Lord created all beings and resides within them as the inner Self; thus, the worthiness of a recipient depends on the degree of realization.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes seeing the divine in all, with gradations based on spiritual evolution.

teachings.teaching: Due to human tendency to disrespect each other, worship of images (arcā) was introduced in Treta Yuga and subsequent ages.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga accepts image worship as a valid and necessary method for the age.

teachings.teaching: Worship of the image is fruitless for those who hate the Lord, but for devotees it is beneficial.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses that attitude is key; devotion transforms ritual into spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Among humans, the brahmana is the best recipient due to tapas, knowledge, and contentment; the brahmana embodies the Veda, which is the Lord's form.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga honors the brahmana as a living embodiment of scripture and a worthy recipient of charity.

teachings.teaching: Brahmanas purify the three worlds with the dust of their feet; they are the great divinity.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the reverence for brahmanas in Kriya-Yoga as channels of grace.

chapter_summary: Narada instructs King Yudhishthira on the proper conduct of śrāddha rituals, emphasizing simplicity, non-violence, and the importance of contentment. He describes the five branches of adharma (vidharma, paradharma, ābhāsa, upamā, chala) and outlines methods to conquer the six enemies (kāma, krodha, lobha, etc.) through self-control and devotion to the guru. The chapter culminates in a detailed allegory of the body as a chariot and the path to liberation through yoga and knowledge.

story.title: Instructions on Dharma and Yoga
story.arc: Narada teaches Yudhishthira about proper rituals, self-control, and the path to liberation.
story.timeline_note: After the war, during Yudhishthira's reign.
story.comic_potential: The allegory of the chariot (verses 41-46) could be visualized as a dramatic battle scene.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the highest happiness; one should not be greedy for wealth or sensual pleasures.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, contentment (santoṣa) is a key niyama that stabilizes the mind for deeper meditation.

teachings.teaching: The five branches of adharma (vidharma, paradharma, ābhāsa, upamā, chala) should be avoided.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes discernment (viveka) to avoid these distortions of dharma, which lead away from self-realization.

teachings.teaching: One should conquer desire by non-impulsion, anger by desirelessness, greed by seeing the drawbacks of wealth, fear by contemplation of truth, grief and delusion by logical inquiry, pride by serving the great, and obstacles in yoga by silence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga prescribes specific techniques (e.g., pranayama, mantra) to overcome these mental afflictions, aligning with the verse's emphasis on self-control.

teachings.teaching: The body is a chariot, the senses are horses, the mind is the reins, and the intellect is the charioteer; the soul is the rider, and the target is the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma uses this allegory to explain the practice of Kriya-Yoga: the yogi must control the senses (horses) through the mind (reins) and intellect (charioteer) to reach the Self.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the guru is the direct means to conquer all obstacles and attain self-realization.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses that guru-bhakti is the foundation of Kriya-Yoga, as the guru imparts the initiatory techniques that lead to liberation.

chapter_summary: Narada instructs King Yudhishthira on the path of liberation through non-duality, karma yoga, and devotion, illustrating with his own past life story. The chapter emphasizes the unity of existence and the importance of surrendering actions to the Supreme.

story.title: Narada's Instructions on Liberation
story.arc: Narada teaches Yudhishthira the path of non-duality and devotion, sharing his own transformation from a gandharva to a sage.
story.timeline_note: Past life of Narada in a previous kalpa; present teaching to Yudhishthira.
story.comic_potential: Narada's fall from gandharva to shudra and rise to brahmin's son due to service.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate reality is non-dual; the world is an illusion superimposed on Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the practice of discerning the witness consciousness from the transient.

teachings.teaching: Karma yoga: surrender all actions to the Supreme with body, mind, and speech.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Kriya-yoga emphasizes offering every action as a sacrifice to the divine, purifying the mind.

teachings.teaching: Devotion and service to the guru and the Lord lead to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Service (seva) is a key component of Kriya-yoga, as seen in Narada's service to brahmins.

teachings.teaching: One should perform prescribed duties without attachment, even as a householder.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Household life is a field for practice; Kriya-yoga integrates action with meditation.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the first Manvantara (Svāyambhuva) and subsequent Manvantaras up to the fourth (Tāmasa). It recounts the lineage of Manus, the appearance of the Lord as Yajña, Vibhu, Satyasena, and Hari, and the protection of dharma. The chapter also includes the prayer of Svāyambhuva Manu and the story of Gajendra's rescue.

story.title: The Manvantaras and the Lord's Incarnations
story.arc: Cosmic history: description of successive Manvantaras and the Lord's appearances to uphold dharma.
story.timeline_note: Spans from the first to the fourth Manvantara, with references to past and future.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic prayers, battles with demons, rescue of Gajendra, and cosmic scale.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner controller who activates the universe but remains unaffected.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Self as the witness (sākṣī) who is distinct from the activities of the mind and body.

teachings.teaching: One should enjoy what is given by the Lord, without coveting others' wealth.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of aparigraha (non-possessiveness) and contentment (santoṣa).

teachings.teaching: The Lord engages in action without attachment, teaching humans to act without ego.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the practice of karma-yoga: performing actions as an offering to the Divine, without attachment to results.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the beautiful Trikuta mountain and its surroundings, including the garden of Varuna called Rtuman, a lake with divine lotuses, and the story of Gajendra (the king of elephants) who, while bathing in the lake with his herd, is attacked by a crocodile. After a long struggle, Gajendra realizes his helplessness and turns to the Supreme Lord for protection.

story.title: Gajendra's Deliverance
story.arc: Gajendra, the king of elephants, is attacked by a crocodile while bathing. After a prolonged struggle, he surrenders to the Supreme Lord, who eventually rescues him.
story.timeline_note: This story is narrated by Śrī Śuka to King Parīkṣit as part of the Eighth Canto of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa.
story.comic_potential: The vivid description of the mountain, lake, and the dramatic struggle between Gajendra and the crocodile offers rich visual and emotional scenes.

teachings.teaching: When in distress, one should give up reliance on material resources and surrender to the Supreme Lord, who is the ultimate protector.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the turning point where the practitioner, after exhausting all personal efforts, surrenders to the divine will, leading to grace.

teachings.teaching: The material world is full of dangers; even the powerful are subject to suffering. Only the Lord can grant true safety.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the body and mind are temporary and subject to afflictions; the soul must seek refuge in the eternal.

chapter_summary: Gajendra, the king of elephants, is caught by a crocodile in a lake. After a long struggle, he prays to the Supreme Lord, offering a profound hymn of surrender. The Lord, hearing his prayer, appears on Garuda and rescues him by killing the crocodile.

story.title: Gajendra's Deliverance
story.arc: Gajendra, trapped by a crocodile, prays to the Lord and is rescued.
story.timeline_note: Occurs during the reign of Svāyambhuva Manu or later; specific epoch not given.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic struggle, desperate prayer, divine intervention with Garuda swooping in.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is the ultimate refuge for all beings in distress.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of total surrender (prapatti) as a means to transcend the ego and attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is beyond all dualities and attributes, yet He manifests through His māyā for the sake of creation and protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the realization of the Lord's transcendental nature (nirguṇa) is achieved through the purification of the mind via Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Devotion and surrender lead to liberation from the cycle of birth and death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that by focusing the mind on the Lord within the heart, one can overcome the bondage of karma.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Gajendra (the king of elephants) and his liberation by Lord Vishnu. It describes how King Indradyumna, a devotee of Vishnu, was cursed by sage Agastya to become an elephant due to a perceived slight. Despite his elephant birth, Indradyumna retained remembrance of Vishnu due to his previous devotion. When attacked by a crocodile (the gandharva Huhu, cursed by Devala), Gajendra prayed to Vishnu, who rescued him and granted him liberation, restoring him to a divine form as a four-armed associate of Vishnu. The chapter also includes a hymn by Lord Vishnu listing his various manifestations and sacred entities, promising liberation to those who remember them at dawn.

story.title: Liberation of Gajendra
story.arc: Gajendra's fall and redemption through devotion
story.timeline_note: The story spans multiple lifetimes: Indradyumna's reign, his curse, elephant birth, attack by crocodile, and final liberation.
story.comic_potential: High - dramatic scenes of elephant king praying, Vishnu's sudden appearance, and transformation.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Vishnu can overcome even the most severe curses and lead to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the power of constant remembrance (smriti) of the divine, which purifies the mind and dissolves karmic bonds.

teachings.teaching: Remembering Vishnu's forms and sacred entities at dawn removes sins and grants liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the practice of morning meditation (pratah smaranam) as a Kriya-Yoga technique to align with the divine and purify the subtle body.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace is available to all who call upon Him with sincerity, regardless of their external form.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that the inner attitude (bhava) is more important than outer rituals; even an elephant's heartfelt prayer is answered.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the sixth manvantara (Cakṣuṣa) and the churning of the ocean by the gods and demons, with Brahmā leading a prayer to the Supreme Lord Viṣṇu for protection and success.

story.title: The Churning of the Ocean and Brahmā's Prayer
story.arc: Cosmic history: sixth manvantara and the churning of the ocean
story.timeline_note: Occurs during the sixth manvantara (Cakṣuṣa), after the curse of Durvāsa and the defeat of the gods.
story.comic_potential: High: the churning of the ocean, the gods and demons working together, the appearance of the Kurma avatar, and Brahmā's dramatic prayer.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is the ultimate refuge for all beings, including gods and demons.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes surrendering the ego and actions to the divine, aligning with the practice of offering all karmas to Īśvara.

teachings.teaching: The Lord assumes various forms (avatars) for the sake of creation, preservation, and dissolution.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees these as manifestations of the divine will, guiding the practitioner to recognize the same consciousness in all forms.

teachings.teaching: Actions offered to the Lord, even if imperfect, are never fruitless.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle of karma-yoga: dedicating all actions to the divine purifies the mind and leads to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Worship of Viṣṇu is equivalent to worship of all beings and the self.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches that serving the divine in all beings is a direct path to self-realization.

chapter_summary: Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas did on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Sanjaya describes Duryodhana's address to Drona, listing the warriors on both sides, the blowing of conches, and the beginning of the battle.

story.title: The Gathering on the Battlefield of Kurukshetra
story.arc: Beginning of the Kurukshetra War
story.timeline_note: The first day of the 18-day war, just before the battle begins.
story.comic_potential: High drama as armies face off, conches blow, and heroes are named.

teachings.teaching: The sacred land of Kurukshetra is a dharmakshetra, a field of righteousness where the battle is not just physical but also a test of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the battlefield represents the inner field of spiritual practice where one must fight the war of self-transformation, with the body as the chariot and the mind as the driver.

teachings.teaching: Duryodhana's anxiety about the Pandava army's strength reflects attachment to material power and fear of loss.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the ego's identification with external outcomes, which must be surrendered in Kriya-Yoga to attain inner peace.

teachings.teaching: The listing of warriors on both sides shows the complexity of dharma, where relatives and teachers are arrayed against each other.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the inner conflict of the practitioner, where various tendencies (vrittis) must be discerned and harmonized through Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Bhishma's lion-like roar and blowing of the conch symbolize the call to action and the awakening of spiritual energy.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the conch represents the pranava (Om) and the roar is the awakening of kundalini, urging the practitioner to engage in the inner battle.

teachings.teaching: Krishna and Arjuna blowing their divine conches signifies the divine and human partnership in the spiritual journey.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Kriya-Yoga practitioner must invoke the divine (Krishna) within to guide the individual self (Arjuna) in the practice.

chapter_summary: The gods, led by Brahma and Shiva, praise Lord Vishnu who appears before them. Vishnu advises them to make peace with the demons and churn the ocean of milk for amrita, promising his help. The demons agree, and they attempt to use Mount Mandara as a churning rod, but it falls and crushes many. Vishnu then lifts the mountain with one hand, places it on Garuda, and carries it to the ocean.

story.title: The Churning of the Ocean of Milk Begins
story.arc: Preparation for the churning
story.timeline_note: After the gods' prayer, Vishnu appears and instructs them to churn the ocean.
story.comic_potential: Vishnu lifting the mountain with one hand and placing it on Garuda is a visually striking scene.

teachings.teaching: Even enemies should be befriended when there is a greater purpose (ahimūṣikavaddevā hy arthasya padavīṃ gataiḥ).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches pragmatic cooperation: aligning with temporary allies for a higher spiritual goal, like using the mind (enemy) as a tool in meditation.

teachings.teaching: All desires are not to be feared; one should not be attached to them (lobhaḥ kāryo na vo jātu roṣaḥ kāmastu vastuṣu).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as: desires (kama) are natural, but one must not be controlled by them; use them as energy for practice.

teachings.teaching: Nothing is achieved by anger; all goals are accomplished by gentleness (na saṃrambheṇa sidhyanti sarvārthāḥ sāntvayā yathā).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of calmness and sattvic approach in spiritual practice over forceful methods.

chapter_summary: The chapter describes the churning of the ocean by the gods and demons, with Vasuki as the rope and Mandara Mountain as the churning rod. Hari (Vishnu) takes the front of the serpent, and the gods follow him.

story.title: The Churning of the Ocean
story.arc: The gods and demons prepare to churn the ocean for nectar.
story.timeline_note: This event occurs during the churning of the ocean, a key episode in the Puranas.
story.comic_potential: The image of gods and demons cooperating with a giant serpent and mountain has visual humor.

teachings.teaching: Cooperation between opposing forces can achieve great goals.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, the Daitya lords reject the churning of the ocean as an act of the Supreme Lord, criticizing the use of the serpent Vasuki as the churning rope. They argue that the serpent's body is inauspicious and that they will not accept it. The chapter contrasts their attitude with that of the devotees who see everything as part of the Lord's plan.

story.title: The Daityas Reject the Churning
