story.timeline_note: During Kṛṣṇa's childhood in Vrindavan, while he is out grazing cows in the forest.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' exaggerated descriptions of nature's reactions to Kṛṣṇa's flute, such as cows forgetting to chew and rivers stopping their flow, have a humorous, whimsical quality.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine sound (flute) to captivate all beings and draw them to the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the flute represents the inner sound (nada) that awakens the soul's longing for union with the divine. The gopis' absorption mirrors the yogi's state of samadhi.

teachings.teaching: Separation (viraha) intensifies love and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the pain of separation is a catalyst for deeper spiritual practice, purifying the heart and leading to ultimate union.

teachings.teaching: Nature itself responds to the presence of the divine, reflecting the interconnectedness of all creation.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of 'sarvam khalv idam brahma' – all is Brahman. The yogi perceives the divine in all phenomena.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme enjoyer and the source of all bliss.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the ultimate goal is to experience the bliss of the Self, which is identical to Kṛṣṇa's joy.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Ariṣṭa in the form of a bull, then Narada informs Kamsa that Krishna and Balarama are the sons of Vasudeva and Devaki, destined to kill him. Kamsa imprisons Vasudeva and Devaki, sends Keśī to kill the brothers, and plots their death through wrestlers and an elephant. He sends Akrūra to bring them to Mathura for a bow sacrifice.

story.title: The Killing of Ariṣṭa and Kamsa's Plot
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan and early encounters with demons, leading to the invitation to Mathura.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the killing of Pralamba and before the Mathura pastimes.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle with a bull demon, Narada's revelation, Kamsa's rage and plotting, and the suspense of Akrūra's journey.

teachings.teaching: Krishna protects his devotees and destroys demons who terrorize them.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the inner purification where the divine force (Krishna) eliminates negative tendencies (demons) that disturb the mind (gokula).

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to the fruits of actions; success and failure depend on destiny.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes detachment from outcomes and surrender to the divine will, as Akrūra advises Kamsa.

teachings.teaching: Even a king like Kamsa, when overcome by anger and fear, acts irrationally and brings about his own destruction.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle that uncontrolled emotions (kama, krodha) lead to bondage and downfall.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Kesi in the form of a horse, then later kills the demon Vyoma (son of Maya) who had kidnapped cowherd boys. The sage Narada visits Krishna, praises him, and prophesies his future deeds.

story.title: Krishna Slays Kesi and Vyoma
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's arrival in Vrindavan and before the killing of Kamsa; Narada's prophecy looks forward to future events.
story.comic_potential: The playful hide-and-seek among cowherd boys, the dramatic horse-demon attack, and the cave rescue offer vivid visual scenes.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys demons effortlessly.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the dissolution of negative karmic impressions (kleshas) through the power of divine consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner self of all beings, the witness in the heart.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that realizing this inner witness (sakshi) is the goal of Kriya-Yoga meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that divine descent (avatara) occurs to restore cosmic balance and guide practitioners.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are eternal and can be witnessed by pure devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that through deep meditation, one can perceive the Lord's eternal lilas.

chapter_summary: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, reflecting on his great fortune to see Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. Upon arrival, he worships their footprints, meets them, and is warmly received. After hospitality, Nanda inquires about Kamsa's tyranny.

story.title: Akrūra's Journey to Gokula
story.arc: Akrūra, sent by Kamsa, travels to Gokula, but his devotion transforms the mission into a pilgrimage. He meets Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, is warmly hosted, and reports Kamsa's atrocities.
story.timeline_note: After Kṛṣṇa's childhood pastimes, before the Mathurā episode.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's internal monologue of ecstasy contrasts with his official role as Kamsa's spy, creating dramatic irony.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord is the highest blessing, even for a lowly person.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the grace of the guru, which is the ultimate purifier.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's feet are worshipped by great beings and grant liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that surrender to the guru's feet is the essence of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial but reciprocates with devotees.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of divine reciprocity through sincere practice.

teachings.teaching: The purpose of human life is to see and serve the Lord, abandoning pride and sorrow.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga practice leads to direct perception of the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrura arrives in Vrindavan to take Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the festival. The gopis lament their impending separation from Krishna. Krishna and Balarama depart with Akrura, and the gopis return home in despair. On the way, they stop at the Yamuna, where Akrura bathes and has a vision of the serpent Ananta Shesha with Krishna and Balarama in his lap.

story.title: Akrura's Arrival and the Gopis' Lament
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Putana and other demons, before the Kamsa's death.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lament and Akrura's underwater vision offer vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna shows that the highest goal is not material gain but devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the state of 'bhakti' where the mind is completely absorbed in the divine, leading to inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's assurance 'I will return' teaches that the divine is always accessible to the sincere devotee.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of 'pratyahara' – the devotee's faith in the guru's promise sustains them during separation.

teachings.teaching: Akrura's vision of Ananta Shesha with Krishna and Balarama reveals the cosmic form of the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this vision symbolizes the awakening of the serpentine energy (kundalini) and the realization of the self as part of the divine.

chapter_summary: Akrura, having seen Krishna and Balarama, is overwhelmed with devotion. He praises the Lord, describing His divine form adorned with the Srivatsa mark, Kaustubha gem, and forest garland, surrounded by associates like Sunanda, Nanda, Sanaka, and great devotees like Prahlada and Narada. Akrura offers a heartfelt prayer with choked voice and tearful eyes.

story.title: Akrura's Vision and Prayer
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna and Balarama's return to Dvaraka, Akrura arrives and sees the Lord.
story.comic_potential: Akrura's overwhelming emotion and choked voice could be depicted humorously as he struggles to speak.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is served by all divine potencies and great devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the integration of all energies (kriyas) in service to the Supreme, leading to inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Devotion leads to ecstatic symptoms like horripilation and tears.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such physical reactions are signs of pranayama and kriya awakening, where the life force responds to divine love.

teachings.teaching: Prayer should be offered with humility and focused mind.
teachings.lens_note: Akrura's posture (bowed head, folded hands) exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga principle of asana and dharana, steadying the body and mind for meditation.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the source of the universe, and the ultimate object of all worship. He describes how all beings, from Brahmā down to the immovable, are bound by the three guṇas, yet Kṛṣṇa remains transcendent. Akrūra confesses his own ignorance and delusion, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Akrūra, having arrived in Vraja, offers a heartfelt prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging His supremacy and seeking refuge.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Vraja, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's ecstatic prayer and listing of Kṛṣṇa's avatars could be depicted as a visionary montage.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa is the ultimate cause of all causes, the source of the universe, and the goal of all paths of worship.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that all practices, whether karma, jñāna, or bhakti, ultimately lead to the same supreme reality, which is the inner self.

teachings.teaching: The three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) bind all beings, but the Lord is beyond them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that through disciplined practice, one can transcend the guṇas and realize the self's identity with the supreme.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's avatars manifest for the protection of the righteous and the destruction of the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that divine incarnations demonstrate the path of dharma and the power of yoga.

teachings.teaching: The jīva is deluded by māyā, identifying with the body and possessions, and suffers in saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that ignorance (avidyā) is the root cause of bondage, and knowledge (vidyā) through yoga leads to liberation.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord is the ultimate means to overcome delusion and attain peace.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender (prapatti) is the culmination of practice, where the individual effort merges with divine grace.

chapter_summary: Akrūra witnesses Kṛṣṇa's divine form in the Yamunā, then returns to Mathurā with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. The brothers enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, and perform feats: killing a washerman, granting boons to a weaver, and accepting garlands from Sudāmā.

story.title: Entry into Mathurā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After leaving Vṛndāvana, before killing Kaṃsa.
story.comic_potential: The washerman's arrogant refusal and swift beheading; the weaver's joyful service and transformation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's form is both immanent and transcendent; Akrūra sees Kṛṣṇa's universal form in water.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this vision represents the realization of the inner Self (ātman) as identical with Brahman, achieved through deep meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is equal to all beings and bestows grace on devotees regardless of status.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine grace is impartial; the weaver and Sudāmā receive blessings due to their devotion, not social standing.

teachings.teaching: Service to the Lord and His devotees purifies one's existence.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga teaches that selfless service (karma-yoga) purifies the mind and leads to liberation.

chapter_summary: Bhagavata Purana Chapter 41 (part 2) describes Krishna granting a boon to a devotee, bestowing prosperity, strength, longevity, fame, and beauty, and then departing with His elder brother.

story.title: Krishna Grants a Boon
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the boon-giving episode, Krishna and Balarama leave.
story.comic_potential: The boon-giving scene could be depicted with divine radiance and blessings.

teachings.teaching: The boon includes friendship with devotees, compassion for all beings, and material blessings like prosperity, strength, longevity, fame, and beauty.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this boon represents the integration of bhakti (devotion) and karma (action), where material prosperity is seen as a means to serve the divine and cultivate compassion.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, and Krishna straightens her body. They then break the great bow in the arena, kill Kaṃsa's guards, and spend the night in the city. Kaṃsa, terrified by omens, arranges a wrestling festival the next day.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to the killing of Kaṃsa
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Krishna and Balarama have arrived in Mathura for the bow sacrifice and before the wrestling match.
story.comic_potential: The straightening of Trivakra's body and the breaking of the bow are visually striking scenes.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's touch straightens the hunchbacked woman, symbolizing spiritual transformation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the straightening of the kundalini and the purification of the subtle body through divine contact.

teachings.teaching: The breaking of the bow signifies the destruction of ego and material pride.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets the bow as the mind's tension; breaking it is the dissolution of mental constructs through yoga.

teachings.teaching: Kaṃsa's omens and fear represent the anxiety of the ego facing its own dissolution.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these omens are signs of the ego's resistance to surrender, which must be overcome.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter the wrestling arena after killing the elephant Kuvalayapida. The people praise them, recounting their divine feats. The wrestler Canura challenges them to a match, and Krishna agrees, emphasizing fair play.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's fall
story.timeline_note: After killing the elephant, Krishna and Balarama enter the wrestling arena; the crowd recounts past deeds; Canura challenges them.
story.comic_potential: High: elephant fight, crowd reactions, dramatic entry.

teachings.teaching: One should please the king by actions, mind, and words, as subjects gain welfare thereby; otherwise, the opposite.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of aligning individual actions with the cosmic order (dharma) to achieve harmony and spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Fair play in combat: wrestling should be conducted with equal strength and without adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes righteous action (kriya) as a means to purify the mind; fair competition mirrors the inner discipline of balancing energies.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat and kill the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the arena of Kamsa. Kamsa orders the arrest of Nanda and Vasudeva, but Krishna leaps onto his platform, grabs him by the hair, and throws him to the ground, killing him. Kamsa's brothers attack and are slain by Balarama. The women of Kamsa's family lament. Krishna and Balarama then release their parents from bondage.

story.title: The Slaying of Kamsa
story.arc: Krishna's Pastimes in Mathura
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Krishna and Balarama have arrived in Mathura and are challenged to wrestle by Kamsa's wrestlers.
story.comic_potential: High: The wrestling match and the dramatic leap onto Kamsa's platform are visually striking. The lament of the women and the liberation of the parents provide emotional depth.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and destroys the wicked. Even when faced with powerful enemies, the devotees of the Lord are safe.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner battle where the practitioner must overcome the wrestlers (sensory distractions) with the help of the divine will (Krishna) and strength (Balarama). The arena is the body, and Kamsa is the ego.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with those who commit adharma. The women in the audience criticize the assembly for allowing the unfair match.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the need to avoid negative influences and to cultivate a sattvic environment for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate controller and the refuge of all beings. Even Kamsa, who constantly thought of Krishna, attained liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle that constant remembrance of the divine, even in enmity, can lead to liberation. In Kriya-Yoga, this is akin to the power of focused awareness.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans who had fled due to Kamsa, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan. They then undergo the sacred thread ceremony and study under Sage Sandipani in Avanti, learning all scriptures and arts in 64 days. To fulfill the guru's fee, they retrieve his son from the ocean, killing the demon Panchajana, and bring the boy back from Yama's city.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life in Mathura and education under Sandipani
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The scene of Krishna and Balarama as students learning all scriptures in 64 days, and the retrieval of the guru's son from the ocean and Yama's city, has humorous and adventurous elements.

teachings.teaching: One must serve and honor one's parents and guru, as Krishna and Balarama exemplify.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the importance of devotion and service (seva) as a means to purify the mind and attain self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The guru's fee must be fulfilled even at great personal risk, as shown by Krishna retrieving Sandipani's son.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of guru-bhakti and the necessity of complete surrender to the guru's command, which in Kriya-Yoga is a key step in spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Even the Lord, when acting as a human, follows the norms of dharma, including undergoing sacred thread ceremony and studying under a guru.
teachings.lens_note: This shows that divine beings set an example for humans; in Kriya-Yoga, it underscores the importance of following scriptural injunctions and the guru's guidance.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, praises the devotion of Nanda and Yashoda, and explains the nature of the Supreme Lord. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's arrival.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and Uddhava's consolation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna killed Kamsa and moved to Mathura, he sends Uddhava to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The emotional reunion and the gopis' morning activities provide visual and dramatic scenes.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is impartial and beyond material distinctions.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of pure consciousness (sattva) where the yogi sees the Self in all.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's incarnations are for the protection of the righteous and for pastime.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that divine play (lila) is a manifestation of the supreme bliss (ananda).

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner controller of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the inner Self (atman) as the witness.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord transcends all material relationships.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that bhakti is the highest yoga, leading to liberation.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense separation and love for Krishna, questioning his faithfulness. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, which includes philosophical teachings on the nature of the self and the importance of constant remembrance. The gopis, though comforted, remain deeply attached to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava is sent to console the gopis.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lamentations and their sharp rebukes of the bee (as a metaphor) could be portrayed with exaggerated emotion.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is pure consciousness, distinct from the body and mind, and is unaffected by the three states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the witness consciousness (sakshi) through meditative practice, where one disidentifies from the fluctuations of the mind.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna (or the divine) is the highest form of devotion and leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes continuous mindfulness (smriti) of the divine as a core practice, akin to the gopis' unwavering focus on Krishna.

teachings.teaching: Detachment from worldly attachments (family, home) is necessary for spiritual progress.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of vairagya (dispassion) as a prerequisite for inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: The highest happiness is in freedom from desire (nairashya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees desire as a binding force; the gopis' statement echoes the teaching that inner peace comes from letting go of expectations.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the gopis and Nanda's family after Krishna's departure. He stays for several months, singing Krishna's pastimes and alleviating their grief. The gopis' intense love for Krishna is praised as the highest devotion. Uddhava returns to Mathura and reports their devotion to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Consolation of the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan and the aftermath
story.timeline_note: After Krishna leaves Vrindavan for Mathura, Uddhava is sent to console the residents.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' intense separation and Uddhava's poetic praise create a poignant, devotional atmosphere.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' love for Krishna is the highest form of devotion, surpassing even the desires of great sages and yogis.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the pinnacle of bhakti where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all worldly attachments and even scriptural injunctions.

teachings.teaching: Even those who are seemingly unqualified (like the gopis) can attain the highest spiritual perfection through pure devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of background, as the gopis exemplify that sincere love for the divine is the sole requirement.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the feet of the devotees (gopis) is supremely purifying.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on the guru's grace and the association of advanced practitioners as a means to accelerate spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The residents of Vrindavan pray for constant remembrance of Krishna in mind, speech, and body.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that all actions should be offered to the divine, integrating devotion into every aspect of life.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the gopī Trivakrā (Kubjā) at her home, fulfills her desires, then goes to Akrūra's house with Balarāma and Uddhava. Akrūra worships them and praises Krishna, who then sends Akrūra to Hastinapura to check on the Pāṇḍavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Trivakrā and Akrūra
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, before the Pāṇḍavas' exile.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's intimate interaction with Trivakrā and the subsequent philosophical discourse with Akrūra.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme cause and pervades all creation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Recognizing the Lord as the inner self (ātman) is the goal of yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is not bound by the guṇas or their actions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The practitioner must transcend the guṇas through disciplined action and devotion.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and destroy adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The descent of the Lord (avatāra) is a model for the yogi's descent into the body for spiritual work.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike holy waters or deities made of clay.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Association with realized souls (satsaṅga) is a powerful means of purification.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura to assess the situation of the Pāṇḍavas. He meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī expresses her sorrow and devotion to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, warning against adharma. Dhṛtarāṣṭra acknowledges his attachment to his sons but submits to fate. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy and Kuntī's lament
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional outburst, and Dhṛtarāṣṭra's stubbornness.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the results of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the solitary nature of spiritual practice; each individual must perform their own kriya to attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired through adharma is taken away by others; one who hoards it is foolish.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views adharma as actions that disturb inner peace; true wealth is spiritual merit gained through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a dream, illusion, or mental construct; one should control the mind and remain equal and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the world is a projection of the mind; through kriya, one withdraws from illusion and attains inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the will of Īśvara, who descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, Īśvara is the inner guide; surrender to the divine will is essential for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his son-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, understanding their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa decimates the enemy forces, while Balarāma captures Jarāsandha but spares him at Kṛṣṇa's request. Jarāsandha returns to Magadha, later attacking seventeen more times, each time defeated. After the eighteenth attack is imminent, the Yavana king Kālayavana, sent by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa builds a fortress city in the sea to protect the Yadus.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attack and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as a king and protector
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa defeats him seventeen times. Before the eighteenth battle, Kālayavana arrives, prompting Kṛṣṇa to build Dvārakā.
story.comic_potential: The repeated defeats of Jarāsandha and his stubbornness, the dramatic arrival of Kālayavana, and the miraculous construction of Dvārakā.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to relieve the earth's burden, protect the righteous, and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of dharma-rakṣaṇa: the divine descends to restore cosmic balance through action.

teachings.teaching: A true hero does not boast but demonstrates prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes kriya (action) over vākya (words); true yoga is in doing, not speaking.

teachings.teaching: One should not kill a surrendered enemy if there is a greater purpose.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of yukta-vairāgya: detachment from personal vengeance for higher good.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's acts, though seemingly human, are divine play (līlā).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views līlā as the spontaneous expression of divine consciousness, beyond karma.

chapter_summary: Krishna builds Dvaraka city with divine gifts from the gods, including Sudharma assembly hall and Parijata tree from Indra, horses from Varuna, and treasures from Kubera. He then leaves the city unarmed with Rama to address the citizens.

story.title: Building Dvaraka and Divine Gifts
story.arc: Krishna establishes his capital and receives boons from the gods, then prepares to address the people.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Dvaraka, before the battle with Jarasandha.
story.comic_potential: The gods lining up to give gifts to Krishna like a divine gift registry.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts (Sudharma, Parijata, horses, treasures) symbolize that a devotee who surrenders to Krishna receives all necessities from the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the fruition of kriya: when one aligns with the divine will, the universe provides the tools for service.

teachings.teaching: Krishna leaves the city unarmed with Rama, showing that true protection comes from divine presence, not weapons.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the state of a yogi who, established in the self, has no need for external defenses; the inner power (Rama as strength) accompanies.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a great king from the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been guarding the gods for a long time. After being granted a boon, he chose to sleep in a cave. The Yavana king, pursuing Krishna, entered the cave and kicked Mucukunda, who then burned him with his angry glance. Krishna revealed himself to Mucukunda, who recognized him as Narayana and offered prayers, realizing the futility of worldly attachments.

story.title: Mucukunda's Deliverance
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes with the Yadus
story.timeline_note: After Krishna killed Kamsa and other demons, the Yavana king attacked Mathura. Krishna fled to a cave where Mucukunda was sleeping.
story.comic_potential: The Yavana's mistaken identity and Mucukunda's sleepy anger provide humorous moments.

teachings.teaching: The world is deluded by Maya and attached to temporary pleasures, neglecting the Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this highlights the need to turn inward and practice kriya to overcome maya.

teachings.teaching: Human birth is rare and should be used to worship the Lord's lotus feet, not to fall into the dark well of household life.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga is the means to utilize this human birth for self-realization.

teachings.teaching: Time, as the Lord, eventually destroys even the most powerful, like a snake swallowing a mouse.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that one must transcend time through spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: King Mucukunda, after being awakened by Kṛṣṇa, recounts his past life as a king who conquered the world but now sees the futility of material pursuits. He praises the value of saintly association and surrenders to Kṛṣṇa, seeking liberation. Kṛṣṇa blesses him with unwavering devotion and promises him a future birth as a brāhmaṇa and ultimate liberation.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Mucukunda, a former king, realizes the futility of material life and surrenders to Kṛṣṇa, who grants him devotion and eventual liberation.
story.timeline_note: After Kṛṣṇa kills Kālayavana and Mucukunda is awakened from his long sleep.
story.comic_potential: Mucukunda's dramatic realization and Kṛṣṇa's compassionate response.

teachings.teaching: The body is ultimately reduced to feces, worms, and ashes, highlighting the impermanence of material existence.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need to transcend identification with the physical body through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Even a king who enjoys worldly pleasures is like a play-deer led by women, indicating the bondage of sense enjoyment.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that sense objects are traps; one must withdraw the senses inward through pratyahara.

teachings.teaching: Performing austerities and giving up enjoyment with desire for future rewards still leads to dissatisfaction.
teachings.lens_note: True happiness comes from desireless action, as in Kriya-Yoga's nishkama karma.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints (sat-sanga) leads to devotion to the Supreme Lord, which is the cause of liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that sat-sanga is crucial for awakening the dormant spiritual energy (kundalini).

teachings.teaching: The loss of kingdom is seen as a blessing because it removes attachments; renunciation is sought even by great kings.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, detachment from material results is essential for progress in meditation.

teachings.teaching: One should not desire anything other than service to the Lord's lotus feet, which is the highest boon.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with bhakti-yoga, which Sharma integrates with Kriya-Yoga as the culmination.

teachings.teaching: Renouncing all desires for material rewards, one should take shelter of the Lord, who is pure consciousness beyond the gunas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga aims at realizing the self as pure consciousness (jñapti-matra) through meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects those who surrender, granting fearlessness and freedom from sorrow.
teachings.lens_note: This is the promise of grace in Kriya-Yoga when one surrenders ego.

teachings.teaching: Unwavering devotion is not disturbed by temptations of boons; it is a sign of spiritual maturity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that steady devotion arises from regular Kriya practice.

teachings.teaching: For non-devotees, even pranayama and other practices cannot eradicate subtle desires; they resurface.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that without devotion, yogic practices are incomplete.

teachings.teaching: One should wander the earth with mind fixed on the Lord, and perform one's duties without attachment.
teachings.lens_note: This is the essence of karma-yoga as taught in Kriya-Yoga: action with awareness of the divine.

teachings.teaching: Even a king who hunts animals can be purified by taking shelter of the Lord and performing penance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga includes purification through mantra and meditation.

teachings.teaching: In the next life, one becomes a friend to all beings and a brāhmaṇa, ultimately attaining the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the progressive nature of spiritual evolution in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the events following Krishna's departure from the cave where he blessed the Ikshvaku king. It describes the king's retreat to Badarikashrama for penance, Krishna's return to Dvaraka, the attack by Jarasandha, the flight of Krishna and Balarama to Mount Pravarṣaṇa, Jarasandha's attempt to burn them, their escape, and the marriage of Krishna to Rukmini. The chapter also includes Rukmini's message to Krishna expressing her desire to marry him and her plan for him to abduct her during a temple festival.

story.title: Krishna's Marriage to Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's return from the cave and before the great war.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic escape from burning mountain, secret message, and daring abduction.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the highest wealth; even a poor contented man sleeps happily, while an discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, santosha (contentment) is a key niyama. This verse emphasizes that inner peace arises from being satisfied with what one has, not from external acquisitions.

teachings.teaching: The wise bow to brahmanas who are content with their lot, friendly to all beings, free from ego, and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on humility and service to the wise. Such sages embody the qualities cultivated through yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: A king is dear to me if his subjects live happily in his realm, protected by him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as a metaphor for the inner ruler (consciousness) that must protect and nurture the subjects (thoughts, senses) for harmony.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's devotion: She considers Krishna as her only refuge and is willing to give up her life if she does not obtain his grace.
teachings.lens_note: This exemplifies the Kriya-Yoga ideal of complete surrender (Ishvara pranidhana) as the highest form of devotion and means to liberation.

chapter_summary: Krishna receives Rukmini's message, confesses his love, and decides to abduct her before her forced marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with a brahmana messenger. Meanwhile, Rukmini's father prepares the wedding, and many enemy kings assemble. Balarama follows with an army. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna, then learns of his arrival. She goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray, accompanied by guards and musicians.

story.title: Rukmini's Message and Krishna's Journey
story.arc: Rukmini's abduction
story.timeline_note: Events occur over a few days: Krishna receives message, travels overnight, arrives on wedding day.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's secret love confession, Rukmini's anxious waiting, the dramatic arrival of enemy kings, and the temple scene with Rukmini's prayer.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's response to Rukmini's love shows that divine love reciprocates human devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'bhakti-yoga' where the devotee's longing attracts the divine response, initiating a dynamic exchange of love.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's prayer to Ambika for Krishna as her husband exemplifies surrender to the divine will.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such prayer aligns the individual will with cosmic purpose, a key step in Kriya-Yoga's path of self-surrender.

teachings.teaching: The assembly of opposing kings represents the obstacles one faces on the spiritual path.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these external enemies symbolize internal vices that must be overcome through disciplined practice and divine grace.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with jewels and a shy smile, walks gracefully to the temple. The assembled kings are captivated and fall from their mounts, overwhelmed by her beauty. She boards Krishna's chariot, and Krishna abducts her before the kings, including Jarāsandha, who are humiliated and enraged.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: After Rukmiṇī's letter to Krishna, she goes to the temple; Krishna abducts her.
story.comic_potential: The kings falling from their mounts in a daze is visually humorous.

teachings.teaching: Divine beauty can overwhelm worldly pride and power.
teachings.lens_note: The kings' infatuation leads to their downfall.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects His devotees and fulfills their desires.
teachings.lens_note: Krishna abducts Rukmiṇī as per her prayer.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama lead the Yadava army against the allied kings who oppose Krishna's marriage to Rukmini. After defeating them, Krishna spares Rukmini's brother Rukmi at her request, but Balarama criticizes the leniency. Balarama then delivers a philosophical discourse on the nature of the self and the illusion of duality.

story.title: Krishna's Marriage to Rukmini and the Defeat of the Allied Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna abducts Rukmini from her svayamvara.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's stern lecture to Krishna after sparing Rukmi, and the comical shaving of Rukmi's head and beard.

teachings.teaching: The self (atman) is distinct from the body; the body is temporary and subject to change, while the self is eternal and unchanging.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes the need to transcend bodily identification through disciplined practice, realizing the self as pure consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Sorrow arises from ignorance of the true self; knowledge of reality dispels it.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework views this as the core of Kriya-Yoga: through meditative insight, one cuts the root of ignorance and attains inner stability.

teachings.teaching: One should not rejoice in victory nor lament in defeat, as all outcomes are governed by time and divine will.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of equanimity, where the practitioner remains steady amidst dualities by resting in the self.

chapter_summary: Krishna abducts Rukmini from her svayamvara, defeats the assembled kings, and returns to Dvaraka where he marries her with great celebration. The defeated kings, especially Rukmi, are humiliated; Rukmi vows not to return to Kundina until he kills Krishna and recovers Rukmini, and builds a city named Bhojakata.

story.title: The Abduction and Marriage of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and the humiliation of Rukmi
story.timeline_note: After the svayamvara, Krishna takes Rukmini to Dvaraka and marries her; Rukmi builds Bhojakata and stays there.
story.comic_potential: Rukmi's dramatic vow and his building of a new city after defeat could be portrayed humorously.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon mental distress and fix the mind on the intellect (buddhi).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this indicates the practice of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and dharana (concentration) to stabilize the mind.

teachings.teaching: The celebration of Krishna's marriage shows the joy of devotees who have exclusive devotion (ananya-bhava) to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such devotion is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner's entire being is offered to the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. Kidnapped as an infant by the demon Shambara, he is swallowed by a fish, later recovered, and raised by Mayavati (Rati in disguise). Upon learning his true identity, he defeats and kills Shambara, then returns to Dvaraka where he is joyfully reunited with his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, growth, and victorious return to Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High; includes a baby swallowed by a fish, a demon cook, a hidden identity, and a dramatic battle with magical weapons.

teachings.teaching: The divine play (lila) of the Lord includes apparent loss and reunion, demonstrating that nothing is truly lost in the spiritual realm.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and the eventual return to the source. Pradyumna's abduction and return symbolize the soul's journey away from and back to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (vidya) overcomes all illusions (maya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the 'Maha-maya' vidya given to Pradyumna represents the supreme knowledge that destroys all ignorance. This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of using inner wisdom to conquer the mind's illusions.

teachings.teaching: Devotion and recognition of the divine in all forms leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Rati's unwavering devotion to Kama (Pradyumna) despite his apparent death mirrors the Kriya-Yoga practitioner's steadfast focus on the inner Self.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel. To clear his name, Krishna tracks the path, finds the jewel in Jambavan's cave, fights Jambavan for 28 days, and is recognized as the Supreme Lord. Jambavan offers his daughter Jambavati and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna returns the jewel to Satrajit, who, repentant, gives his daughter Satyabhama and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna accepts Satyabhama but returns the jewel to Satrajit.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna's divine play to clear his name and marry Satyabhama and Jambavati
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka, after the killing of Kamsa but before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The mistaken identity of Krishna as the Sun god, the 28-day wrestling match with Jambavan, and the dramatic return from the cave.

teachings.teaching: The Lord clears false accusations through righteous action, not by force.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma-shuddhi: purifying one's actions and reputation through selfless effort, not by defending ego.

teachings.teaching: The jewel (Syamantaka) symbolizes material wealth; attachment to it leads to suffering, but offering it to the Lord brings peace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees the jewel as a metaphor for the mind's attachment to objects. Surrendering it to Krishna represents offering one's actions and possessions to the divine, achieving inner peace.

teachings.teaching: Repentance and giving up pride (as Satrajit did) lead to divine grace.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and surrender, leading to purification of the heart.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller and the source of all power; even great beings like Jambavan recognize Him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary highlights that true knowledge (jnana) arises when the ego is subdued, as Jambavan's pride was broken in battle, leading to realization.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura after the Pandavas' house fire, then return to Dvaraka. Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, is killed by Krishna, but the jewel is missing. Akrura is suspected and later reveals the jewel, clearing his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel and the Murder of Satrajit
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Pandavas' escape from the lac house, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: chase scene, jewel theft, mistaken identity, dramatic reveal.

teachings.teaching: Even the Lord undergoes suffering to teach dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this shows that the divine descends to demonstrate how to act in the world while remaining unattached.

teachings.teaching: The wise do not oppose the will of the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes surrender to the divine will as a key practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are for the purification of the world.
teachings.lens_note: The Syamantaka story illustrates how even apparent conflicts serve a higher purpose.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (daughter of Surya), and later marries Mitravinda and Satya (Nagnajiti) after subduing seven fierce bulls. The chapter also recounts Arjuna's burning of the Khandava forest and the gifts from Agni and Maya.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Khandava Forest Episode
story.arc: Krishna's visit to Indraprastha and his marriages to Kalindi, Mitravinda, and Satya, with interlude of Arjuna's forest adventure.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's establishment in Dvaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna splitting into seven forms to subdue bulls is a visually striking scene.

teachings.teaching: Krishna as the supreme lord removes the sufferings of those who remember him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the practice of constant remembrance (smarana) as a means to purify the heart and realize the self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-sufficient and acts out of love, not need.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine actions are spontaneous expressions of bliss (ananda), not motivated by lack.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries many wives, including Satya (daughter of Nagnajit), Bhadra (daughter of Srutakirti), and Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king), after defeating various kings. He also rescues many women from the demon Bhauma.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and Rescue of Women
story.arc: Krishna marries multiple princesses and rescues women from the demon Bhauma.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's establishment in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's multiple marriages and the defeat of Bhauma could be depicted as a series of adventurous episodes.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages demonstrate his role as the supreme enjoyer and protector of devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'bhoga' (enjoyment) as a path to liberation when performed with detachment and devotion.

teachings.teaching: The defeat of Bhauma and rescue of women shows Krishna's compassion and power to liberate the oppressed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the removal of obstacles (bhauma = earthly) through divine grace, leading to spiritual freedom.

chapter_summary: Krishna, urged by Indra and accompanied by Satyabhama, travels to the city of Pragjyotisha to kill the demon Narakasura. He defeats Mura and his seven sons, then kills Narakasura. The Earth goddess (Bhumi) offers Narakasura's earrings and other treasures to Krishna, who then frees 16,000 captive princesses, marries them, and returns to Dvaraka with the Parijata tree.

story.title: The Slaying of Narakasura and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Satyabhama and before the Parijata tree incident.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes, Krishna's multiple forms marrying 16,000 princesses, and the humorous pursuit of bees following the Parijata tree.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's power is absolute; he effortlessly defeats demons and protects devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the practitioner's divine will (Krishna) overcomes the demonic tendencies (Narakasura) through disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord (prapatti) brings liberation and protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Earth's plea (Bhumi) symbolizes the devotee's surrender, which activates the Lord's grace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate reality beyond all material distinctions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees Bhumi's hymn as a meditation on the non-dual self (advaita), where the practitioner realizes the unity of all existence in Krishna.

chapter_summary: Krishna teases Rukmini about her choice of him as husband, pretending to be unworthy. Rukmini responds with a passionate defense of her devotion, explaining that she chose him despite his apparent poverty because he is the Supreme Lord. Krishna then consoles her, praising her devotion and explaining the nature of true love.

story.title: Krishna's Teasing and Rukmini's Devotion
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, early in their married life.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful teasing of Rukmini, pretending to be a poor and unworthy husband, while Rukmini earnestly defends her choice, creates a humorous and tender scene.

teachings.teaching: True devotion is not based on external wealth or status but on love for the Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that the practitioner should not be attached to material gains but should focus on inner devotion and surrender to the divine, which is the essence of kriya-yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-sufficient and does not need anything; he is the ultimate goal of all desires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights that the ultimate reality (Brahman) is complete in itself, and the practice of kriya-yoga aims to realize this self-sufficiency within oneself.

teachings.teaching: A devotee should not be swayed by the opinions of others but remain steadfast in their love for God.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of inner stability (sthira-buddhi) where the practitioner remains unshaken by external circumstances.

teachings.teaching: Those who seek material benefits from the Lord are misguided; true devotees seek only the Lord himself.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the distinction between sakama (desire-ridden) and nishkama (desireless) action; the latter is the path to liberation.

chapter_summary: Rukmini expresses her love and grievances to Krishna, recalling how he abducted her at her svayamvara, and Krishna responds with playful and affectionate words, demonstrating his divine nature while acting like a human husband.

story.title: Rukmini's Loving Complaints and Krishna's Playful Response
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a householder
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their domestic life in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: High - Rukmini's mock anger and Krishna's teasing replies create a playful, romantic comedy.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, though self-satisfied, engages in human-like relationships to teach the path of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine love (prema) is the highest yoga, where the soul (jiva) playfully interacts with the Divine (Krishna) through loving emotions, transcending mere ritual.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's actions as a householder demonstrate that one can live in the world while remaining spiritually detached.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that household life (grihastha) can be a path to liberation when performed as an offering to the Divine, with inner detachment.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens, describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana (Rukmi's daughter), and narrates the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, which ends with Balarama killing Rukmi for his arrogance.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Dice Game with Rukmi
story.arc: Krishna's family life and the consequences of pride
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and other queens, their sons are born. Later, Aniruddha marries Rocana, and the dice game occurs during the wedding festivities.
story.comic_potential: The dice game and Balarama's violent reaction to Rukmi's insults have dramatic and humorous elements, especially the knocking out of Kalinga's teeth.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's queens and sons are manifestations of his divine opulence, yet they are not separate from him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that all beings are expressions of the one consciousness, and attachment to them should be seen as devotion to the divine.

teachings.teaching: Pride and deceit lead to downfall, as seen in Rukmi's arrogance and his subsequent death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that ego (ahamkara) is the root of all conflict; Rukmi's pride in his dice skills and his insult to Balarama represent the ego's resistance to divine will.

teachings.teaching: The sage-like vision of yogis transcends time and space, seeing past, present, and future.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's goal of attaining direct perception of reality through meditation, beyond the limitations of the senses.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Ūṣā, daughter of Bāṇa. Śuka narrates that Bāṇa, a devotee of Śiva, received a boon of a thousand arms and became arrogant. Śiva prophesied his pride would be crushed by a match. Ūṣā dreams of Aniruddha, and her friend Citralekhā, a yoginī, identifies him and brings him to Śoṇitapura. They marry secretly. Bāṇa discovers them and attacks Aniruddha, who fights back. Bāṇa binds Aniruddha with serpent ropes. Ūṣā laments.

story.title: The Marriage of Aniruddha and Ūṣā
story.arc: Bāṇa's pride leads to conflict; divine intervention through love and war.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Vāmana's incarnation; during Kṛṣṇa's time.
story.comic_potential: Romantic dream, secret marriage, comedic guards, dramatic battle.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall; even devotees can become arrogant.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, pride (ahaṅkāra) is a binding force that must be dissolved through surrender to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends social boundaries; Ūṣā's dream leads to union with Aniruddha.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the soul's longing for the Divine is like Ūṣā's dream; it is a call from the inner self that leads to ultimate union.

teachings.teaching: The power of yoga (Citralekhā's yogic ability) enables the fulfillment of divine will.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the practical application of yogic techniques to realize the divine plan; Citralekhā's yoga is a metaphor for disciplined spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna and Balarama lead the Vrishnis to rescue Aniruddha from Bana's city. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, and between their respective armies. Krishna defeats Shiva's forces, cuts off Bana's thousand arms except four, and spares Bana due to Shiva's plea and a boon to Prahlada. Bana surrenders and returns Aniruddha with his wife Usha.

story.title: The Rescue of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's abduction by Usha; four months pass before the Vrishnis learn of it from Narada.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, and fevers; Bana's thousand arms cut off; Kotara's naked intervention; humorous surrender of Shiva's fever.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Lord is the source of all, including time, karma, and the elements. One should surrender to Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the recognition of the Self as the witness (sakshi) beyond all modifications. The fever's prayer (bhp_10.63.025-026) describes the interplay of prakriti and purusha, which the yogi transcends through Kriya.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine descent (avatara) is a model for the yogi's own descent into action with detachment. Krishna's role as 'bhara-hara' (remover of burden) mirrors the Kriya practitioner's removal of karmic impressions.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the Lord's feet, having obtained a human birth, is pitiable and cheats himself.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes Sharma's emphasis on the rare opportunity of human birth for Kriya practice. The senses (indriyas) must be controlled to avoid the 'viṣa' (poison) of sense objects and attain 'amṛta' (nectar) of self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole refuge; even great beings like Brahma and Shiva surrender to Him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that all powers (siddhis) come from the Supreme. The devotee's humility, as shown by Shiva, is essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's mercy is unconditional; He spares even a demon like Bana when a devotee intercedes.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya principle of grace (prasada) descending when the ego is humbled. Bana's pride is cut (arms cut) but he is saved due to Prahlada's boon and Shiva's plea.

chapter_summary: After defeating the demon Bāṇa and receiving blessings from Lord Śiva, Kṛṣṇa returns to his capital Dvārakā with his wives, entering the city amidst grand celebrations. The chapter concludes with a verse stating that one who recites this account of Kṛṣṇa's victory over Śiva in battle will never face defeat.

story.title: Kṛṣṇa's Return to Dvārakā After Defeating Śiva
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Bāṇa and Śiva, Kṛṣṇa returns to Dvārakā.
story.comic_potential: Triumphant return with divine blessings and grand celebrations.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the account of Kṛṣṇa's victory over Śiva ensures freedom from defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of remembrance (smaraṇa) as a yogic practice to overcome obstacles and attain victory in spiritual life.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a generous king who gave away millions of cows to brāhmaṇas, was cursed to become a lizard due to a mistake involving a cow that belonged to two brāhmaṇas. After a long period as a lizard in a well, he was rescued by Lord Kṛṣṇa, who restored his divine form. Nṛga then ascended to heaven. Kṛṣṇa used this incident to teach the Yadus about the severe consequences of misappropriating brāhmaṇa property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's establishment in Dvārakā, during his earthly pastimes.
story.comic_potential: High: A giant lizard in a well, rescued by a divine hero, with a flashback to a generous king's mistake and a moral lesson about karma.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriating brāhmaṇa property, even unknowingly, leads to severe karmic consequences, including hellish suffering for many years.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the principle of karma and the importance of purity in action (kriya). The story illustrates how even a well-intentioned act can have unintended consequences if not performed with full awareness and adherence to dharma.

teachings.teaching: One should never harm a brāhmaṇa, even if he is at fault; instead, one should bow to him always.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as a call to cultivate humility and respect for spiritual authority, recognizing that true power lies in spiritual advancement, not material status.

teachings.teaching: The example of Nṛga shows that even a great king who performed immense charity can fall due to a single mistake, highlighting the need for constant vigilance in dharma.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on sustained practice and awareness, as even advanced practitioners can slip if they become complacent.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Nanda's Gokula, is warmly received by the gopas and gopis, who express longing for Krishna. He stays for two months, enjoying the Yamuna forest with the gopis. The river goddess Varuni appears, and Balarama drinks. In a drunken state, he summons the Yamuna river to bathe; when she disobeys, he drags her with his plow. She submits, and he releases her, then bathes. The chapter ends noting the visible trace of the Yamuna's course as evidence of Balarama's power.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vraja and the Yamuna's Submission
story.arc: Balarama's pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: After Krishna and Balarama killed Kamsa and moved to Mathura, Balarama returns to Vraja for a visit.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken antics, the gopis' emotional outbursts, and the river being dragged by a plow offer vivid visual comedy.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna, expressed through weeping and remembering his pastimes, illustrates the pinnacle of devotional attachment.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the stage of bhava (intense emotion) where the practitioner's heart melts in remembrance of the divine, leading to purification and union.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's summoning of the Yamuna and her submission demonstrates the Lord's absolute control over nature, yet he responds to humble supplication.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as a metaphor for the yogi's mastery over the elements (prana and mind) through Kriya, but also the necessity of surrender (prapatti) to receive grace.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' complaint that Krishna abandoned them despite their sacrifices teaches that divine love is not transactional; the devotee must love without expectation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to nishkama karma (desireless action) and the cultivation of unconditional devotion, where the practitioner offers all actions without attachment to results.

chapter_summary: Paundraka, the king of Karusha, falsely claims to be Vasudeva (Krishna) and sends a messenger demanding Krishna surrender his insignia. Krishna kills Paundraka and his ally the king of Kashi in battle. The king of Kashi's son Sudakshina performs a ritual to invoke a fiery demon, but Krishna's Sudarshana chakra destroys the demon and burns Varanasi.

story.title: The Killing of Paundraka and the Burning of Varanasi
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the Supreme Lord defending his position and devotees
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's return to Dvaraka from Nanda-vraja (referenced in bhp_10.65.034).
story.comic_potential: High; includes a false claimant, a decapitation, a fiery demon, and a divine chakra burning a city.

teachings.teaching: False pride and imitation of the divine lead to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of ego (ahamkara) claiming identity with the Self (Atman) without actual realization. Paundraka's false claim is like a practitioner mistaking a glimpse for full enlightenment.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys evil.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the divine (Krishna) as the inner witness (sakshi) intervenes when the devotee's practice is threatened by external or internal obstacles.

teachings.teaching: Rituals performed with wrong intent (abhicara) backfire on the performer.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the law of karma: actions motivated by hatred or revenge rebound. In Kriya-Yoga, impure intentions during practice create negative samskaras that hinder progress.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates the story of Dvivida, a powerful monkey and friend of Naraka, who terrorized the earth in revenge for Naraka's death. Dvivida disrupted sacrifices, imprisoned people, and finally confronted Balarāma on Raivataka mountain. After a fierce battle, Balarāma killed Dvivida with his fist, restoring peace.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Naraka's death by Kṛṣṇa, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's lewd gestures and Balarāma's drunken state provide comedic contrast to the serious battle.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects dharma by destroying evil forces that disrupt peace and piety.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the necessity of removing internal obstacles (vikṣepas) through disciplined action, as Balarāma's external battle mirrors the inner struggle against impurities.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Jambavati and Krishna, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura to negotiate but is insulted. He becomes furious and drags the city of Hastinapura toward the Ganges with his plow. The Kauravas surrender and offer gifts, and Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the events of the Mahabharata war? Actually, this is before the war, as Duryodhana is alive and ruling.
story.comic_potential: Balarama dragging the entire city of Hastinapura with his plow is a visually humorous and dramatic scene.

teachings.teaching: The anger of a saintly person is for the sake of instructing the foolish, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle that even negative emotions like anger can be channeled as a teaching tool when rooted in compassion and the desire for others' growth.

teachings.teaching: One should not be proud of one's position or power, as everything is under the control of the Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on humility and surrender to the divine will, recognizing that all achievements are by grace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate shelter and controller of all, and even the demigods obey His orders.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights the importance of recognizing the omnipotence of the divine and the futility of egoistic pride.

chapter_summary: Balarama returns to Dvaraka after his pilgrimage, recounts his adventures to the Yadava assembly, and the city still bears marks of his prowess.

story.title: Balarama's Return and Narration
story.arc: Balarama returns to Dvaraka after his pilgrimage and recounts his adventures to the Yadava assembly.
story.timeline_note: After the pilgrimage described in previous chapters.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's dramatic recounting of his exploits, with the city still showing signs of his strength.

teachings.teaching: The importance of sharing one's experiences with the community.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of satsanga (association with truth) and the sharing of spiritual experiences to inspire others.

chapter_summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,000 wives. He witnesses Krishna in each palace, engaged in different activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, marveling at his divine play.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Krishna's Palaces
story.arc: Krishna's divine play (lila) demonstrating simultaneous presence in multiple forms
story.timeline_note: After Narada's previous visit to Dvaraka, he returns to see Krishna's household life.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered reactions as he sees Krishna in different activities in each palace.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple forms demonstrates his yoga-maya, the divine power to be everywhere at once.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'eka-vapu' (one body) manifesting as many, akin to the yogi's ability to be present in multiple states of consciousness simultaneously.

teachings.teaching: Narada's humility and devotion: he bows to Krishna and considers himself blessed to see his lila.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true knowledge arises from devotion and service, not mere intellectual inquiry.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's statement: 'I am the speaker, doer, and approver of dharma; I teach by example.'
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that the guru embodies the teachings; one should not be disturbed by apparent contradictions in divine actions.

chapter_summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dvaraka, including his morning meditation, worship, and court activities. A messenger from imprisoned kings arrives seeking help against Jarāsandha. Nārada appears and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's plan to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Krishna consults Uddhava.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Imprisoned Kings' Plea
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice and the eventual defeat of Jarāsandha.
story.timeline_note: Set in Dvaraka, after Krishna's return from various pastimes, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful morning interactions with his queens, the dramatic entrance of the messenger, and Nārada's sudden appearance.

teachings.teaching: The importance of daily spiritual routine: meditation, worship, and charity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes disciplined daily practice (sādhana) to purify the mind and realize the self.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Lord is the inner controller and the goal of all sacrifices.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees all actions as offerings to the divine, leading to inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: Hearing, chanting, and meditating on the Lord purify even the fallen.
teachings.lens_note: These are key practices in Kriya-Yoga for cleansing the mind and attaining liberation.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. The Yādava army travels to Indraprastha, where Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas welcome them. Krishna stays for several months, enjoying pastimes with Arjuna.

story.title: Uddhava's Advice and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return from the battle with Bāṇa; before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The grand procession of the Yādava army, the emotional reunion of Krishna with the Pāṇḍavas, and the humorous scene of women rushing to see Krishna.

teachings.teaching: Killing Jarāsandha is necessary for the Rājasūya sacrifice and for the welfare of many.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the necessity of removing obstacles (avidyā) through decisive action (kriyā) to achieve spiritual sovereignty.

teachings.teaching: One should use strategy and righteousness to overcome powerful enemies.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the means (Bhīma in disguise) must be aligned with dharma, reflecting the yoga principle of using the body (Bhīma) as an instrument under divine guidance (Krishna).

teachings.teaching: The Lord's presence brings joy and liberation to devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets the reunion as the soul's recognition of the Self (Krishna) leading to bliss and freedom from worldly delusion.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira proposes to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers in four directions. They bring tribute. Jarāsandha, the powerful king of Magadha, remains unsubdued. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna disguise as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja, and challenge Jarāsandha to a duel. After a long fight, Bhīma kills Jarāsandha by splitting his body. Kṛṣṇa releases the imprisoned kings and installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king.

story.title: The Killing of Jarāsandha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile and before the great war.
story.comic_potential: High: Disguise, duel, dramatic splitting of body.

teachings.teaching: One should perform righteous acts like the Rājasūya to attain glory.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practice of selfless action (karma-yoga) for purification.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and bestows grace according to one's devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the divine responds to the sincere effort of the practitioner, not to external status.

teachings.teaching: Even the body is impermanent; one should use it to attain eternal fame through righteous deeds.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that the body is a tool for spiritual evolution, not an end in itself.

teachings.teaching: A true king or warrior should be willing to give even his life for the sake of brāhmaṇas (or higher principles).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the surrender of the ego (the 'king') to the higher Self (the 'brāhmaṇa').

chapter_summary: Krishna releases the kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise him and renounce worldly attachments. Krishna instructs them on devotion and dharma, then sends them back to their kingdoms after honoring them. He then has Bhima kill Jarasandha and returns to Indraprastha with Arjuna and Sahadeva, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

story.title: Liberation of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Jarasandha's defeat and before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic release of the kings, their heartfelt praise, and Krishna's compassionate instruction.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and power lead to pride and downfall; true happiness is in devotion to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This teaching emphasizes the need to transcend material ego through disciplined action and devotion, aligning with the path of karma-yoga.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a mirage; one should not be attached to temporary things.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This echoes the practice of viveka (discrimination) and vairagya (detachment), essential for inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Perform your duties with devotion to Krishna, and you will attain liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the core of karma-yoga: acting without attachment, offering results to the divine.

chapter_summary: Yudhishthira, having heard of Krishna's slaying of Jarasandha, praises Krishna as the Supreme Lord. He then performs the Rajasuya sacrifice with many sages and kings in attendance. During the ceremony, Sahadeva proposes that Krishna be given the first honor. Shishupala objects, insulting Krishna, and is slain by Krishna's discus. Shishupala's soul merges into Krishna. The sacrifice concludes, and Krishna returns to Dvaraka.

story.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and the Slaying of Shishupala
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the Supreme Lord
story.timeline_note: After the slaying of Jarasandha, Yudhishthira performs the Rajasuya sacrifice. Shishupala's death occurs during the ceremony.
