story.comic_potential: A visual guide to each siddhi with symbolic representations, showing the yogi's mind fixed on different aspects of the divine.

teachings.teaching: The eight primary siddhis (aṇimā, mahimā, laghimā, prāpti, prākāmya, īśitā, vaśitā, yat-kāma-avasāyitā) are attained by fixing the mind on specific cosmic principles (tan-mātra, mahat-tattva, paramāṇu, ahaṃ-tattva, sūtra, Viṣṇu as time, Nārāyaṇa as turīya, and nirguṇa Brahman respectively).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these correspond to progressive stages of pratyāhāra and dhāraṇā, where the yogi withdraws from gross elements and merges into subtler aspects of consciousness, ultimately realizing the self as identical with Krishna.

teachings.teaching: The ten secondary siddhis (anūrmimattva, dūra-śravaṇa-darśana, mano-java, kāma-rūpa, para-kāya-praveśana, svacchanda-mṛtyu, devānāṃ saha-krīḍānudarśana, yathā-saṅkalpa-saṃsiddhi, ājñāpratihatā gati, tri-kāla-jñatva, advandva, para-cittādy-abhijñatā, agny-arkāmbu-viṣādīnāṃ pratiṣṭambha, aparājaya) are attained through specific dhāraṇās on aspects of Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views these as natural byproducts of deep samādhi, not goals. They arise when the yogi's prāṇa and mind are fully controlled and merged in Krishna.

teachings.teaching: All siddhis are obstacles (antarāya) for the yogi seeking ultimate union with Krishna; they are mere distractions that waste time.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Kriya-Yoga practitioner should not be sidetracked by powers but remain focused on the inner light and sound, aiming for kaivalya.

teachings.teaching: Krishna alone is the source, lord, and controller of all siddhis, yoga, sāṅkhya, dharma, and the knowledge of Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that all spiritual paths culminate in surrender to the divine, and that Kriya-Yoga is a direct method to realize Krishna as the inner self.

teachings.teaching: The yogi who has attained the state of being fixed in Krishna (mad-bhāva) cannot be hindered by anything; his command is like Krishna's own.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the state of jīvanmukta, where the yogi's will is in perfect harmony with the divine will, and all actions are spontaneous and effortless.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna, as the supreme being, enumerates his divine manifestations (vibhutis) across various categories of existence, explaining that all glories and powers are his expansions. He concludes by instructing Uddhava on the practice of yoga—controlling speech, mind, and senses—to attain liberation.

story.title: The Divine Manifestations (Vibhuti Yoga)
story.arc: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain his manifestations; Krishna responds by listing his vibhutis and concludes with yoga instructions.
story.timeline_note: After the Mahabharata war, Krishna instructs Uddhava.
story.comic_potential: A cosmic catalog of divine identities, with Krishna as the ultimate source.

teachings.teaching: The supreme being is the source of all manifestations; everything exists in him.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to recognizing the inner self as the substratum of all phenomena, leading to non-attachment.

teachings.teaching: Control of speech, mind, and senses is essential for spiritual progress.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga involves systematic control of prana and mind; this verse directly supports that practice.

teachings.teaching: Without controlling the senses, spiritual practices are futile.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that external practices without inner discipline are like water in an unbaked pot.

chapter_summary: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain the eternal dharma characterized by devotion, which was previously taught by Krishna in his Hamsa incarnation to Brahma. Krishna responds by describing the original varṇa system in Kṛta-yuga, the emergence of the four varṇas and āśramas from his body, the natural qualities of each varṇa, and the duties of a brahmacārī, including respect for the guru, celibacy, and study. He also discusses the duties of a gṛhastha, the means of livelihood for brāhmaṇas, and the conduct of a king.

story.title: The Eternal Dharma of Varṇāśrama
story.arc: Uddhava inquires about the dharma that leads to devotion, and Krishna explains the varṇāśrama system and its duties.
story.timeline_note: Set after Krishna's previous teachings to Brahma in the Hamsa form; current time is the Dvāpara or Kali Yuga.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily didactic discourse.

teachings.teaching: The original dharma in Kṛta-yuga was characterized by the 'haṃsa' varṇa, where all were naturally perfect and devoted.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the state of innate purity and effortless alignment with the divine, before the need for structured practices.

teachings.teaching: The four varṇas (brāhmaṇa, kṣatriya, vaiśya, śūdra) and four āśramas (brahmacarya, gṛhastha, vānaprastha, sannyāsa) originate from different parts of the cosmic person.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a map of the human constitution: the head (brāhmaṇa), arms (kṣatriya), thighs (vaiśya), and feet (śūdra) correspond to different faculties and duties in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: The natural qualities of each varṇa are described: brāhmaṇas have śama, dama, etc.; kṣatriyas have tejas, bala, etc.; vaiśyas have āstikya, dāna-niṣṭhā, etc.; śūdras have śuśrūṣā, santoṣa, etc.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these qualities are not mere social labels but inherent tendencies that must be purified and directed toward devotion through Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The common dharma for all varṇas includes ahiṃsā, satya, asteya, akāma-krodha-lobhatā, and bhūta-priya-hitehā.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees these as foundational ethical precepts that prepare the mind for deeper meditation and union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: A brahmacārī should serve the guru, study the Vedas, observe celibacy, and see the guru as non-different from the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights the guru as the embodiment of the divine; service and surrender to the guru are essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: After completing brahmacarya, one may enter gṛhastha, vānaprastha, or sannyāsa, but should not deviate from the path of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that the householder stage is a valid path for spiritual practice when performed with detachment and dedication to God.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa should avoid accepting gifts (pratigraha) as it diminishes austerity, and should live by śiloñcha (gleaning grains) or other pure means.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a call to simplicity and self-reliance, reducing material dependencies to focus on inner purification.

teachings.teaching: A king should protect his subjects like a father, and by doing so, he attains the same world as Indra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the duty of a leader to create conditions conducive to spiritual practice for all.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (11.17.52-58) discusses detachment from family life, emphasizing that one should not be attached to household affairs. It compares family relationships to a traveler's meeting, which ends with death. The wise person lives like a guest, free from possessiveness and ego. Those attached to family and wealth become deluded and suffer, entering darkness after death.

story.title: Detachment from Family Life
story.arc: Teaching on renunciation and the illusory nature of family bonds.
story.timeline_note: Part of Krishna's instructions to Uddhava in the Uddhava Gita.
story.comic_potential: Low; philosophical discourse.

teachings.teaching: Do not be attached to family; do not be negligent even as a householder. The wise see the impermanent as impermanent, even if unseen.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes constant awareness of the transient nature of worldly life, encouraging inner detachment while performing duties.

teachings.teaching: Relationships with sons, wives, and friends are like a traveler's meeting; they separate at death, like a dream after sleep.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a reminder that all relationships are temporary, urging the practitioner to focus on the eternal self.

teachings.teaching: Reflecting thus, one should live in the home like a guest, free from possessiveness and ego, not bound by household affairs.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on non-attachment and living in the world without being of it.

teachings.teaching: A devotee may worship Me through householder duties, then retire to the forest or renounce. But one attached to home, driven by desires for children and wealth, becomes bound by 'I' and 'mine'.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would stress that even householder life can be a path if done with devotion and detachment, but attachment leads to bondage.

teachings.teaching: The deluded person worries about his aged parents, wife, and children, thinking they cannot live without him. Thus his heart is pierced by household anxieties, and after death he enters blinding darkness.
teachings.lens_note: This warns against excessive worry and attachment, which Kriya-Yoga sees as obstacles to spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna describes the duties and practices of the vānaprastha (forest-dwelling) and sannyāsa (renounced) orders, emphasizing detachment, self-control, and devotion to Him as the ultimate goal. He outlines the lifestyle of a forest hermit, the conduct of a renunciant, and the importance of transcending external forms to realize the self's unity with Brahman.

story.title: The Duties of the Forest Hermit and the Renunciant
story.arc: Krishna instructs Uddhava on the progressive stages of āśrama dharma, focusing on vānaprastha and sannyāsa as paths to liberation through detachment and devotion.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gītā discourse before Krishna's departure from the world.
story.comic_potential: A serene teaching scene with Krishna describing the austere life of a hermit, contrasting with the earlier battlefield setting.

teachings.teaching: A vānaprastha should live in the forest, subsist on wild roots and fruits, wear bark or deerskin, and perform austerities like the five fires in summer and standing in water in winter.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these external austerities symbolize the internal discipline of purifying the senses and mind through regulated practice.

teachings.teaching: The forest hermit should perform sacrifices using forest produce, not animal sacrifices, and continue agnihotra and other rituals.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true sacrifice is the offering of one's ego and desires, not external objects.

teachings.teaching: By such penance, the sage becomes emaciated and attains the world of the ṛṣis, ultimately reaching Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: The emaciation represents the reduction of karmic impressions, leading to the realization of the self as non-different from Krishna.

teachings.teaching: One who performs severe penance for petty desires is foolish.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma warns against using spiritual practices for material gain; the goal must be liberation.

teachings.teaching: When unable to perform austerities due to old age, one should merge the fires into oneself and enter fire with mind fixed on Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: This symbolizes the final absorption of the individual consciousness into the cosmic consciousness through Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: When detachment from worldly results arises, one should renounce and become a sannyāsī.
teachings.lens_note: Detachment is the natural outcome of sustained Kriya practice, leading to the renunciation of all actions.

teachings.teaching: After performing sacrifices as prescribed, giving all possessions to the priest, and merging the fires into the self, one should wander without attachment.
teachings.lens_note: The merging of fires symbolizes the withdrawal of prana into the self, a key Kriya technique.

teachings.teaching: The gods, in the form of wife and children, create obstacles for the renunciant, fearing he will attain the supreme.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the mind's attachments (gods) resisting the soul's journey inward.

teachings.teaching: A sannyāsī should wear only a loincloth, carry a staff and water pot, and not accept other possessions except in emergencies.
teachings.lens_note: Minimal possessions reflect the internal state of non-attachment, essential for Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: He should purify his steps by looking, water by straining, speech by truth, and mind by purity.
teachings.lens_note: These are external practices that support internal purification, aligning with Kriya's emphasis on mindfulness.

teachings.teaching: Silence, non-exertion, and control of breath are the staffs for speech, body, and mind; without these, one is not a true yati.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma identifies these as the three components of Kriya: mauna (inner silence), anīhā (effortlessness), and āyāma (breath control).

teachings.teaching: He should beg from all four varṇas, avoiding the fallen, and be content with whatever is obtained.
teachings.lens_note: Contentment is a key attitude in Kriya, reducing mental agitation.

teachings.teaching: After bathing, he should silently eat the purified remnants, sharing with others.
teachings.lens_note: Eating with awareness and gratitude is a form of meditation in Kriya.

teachings.teaching: He should wander alone, with senses controlled, self-content, and seeing equality everywhere.
teachings.lens_note: Solitude and equanimity are prerequisites for deep Kriya practice.

teachings.teaching: Taking shelter in solitude, with a pure mind, he should contemplate the self as non-different from Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya: realizing the identity of the individual self with the Supreme Self.

teachings.teaching: He should investigate bondage and liberation through knowledge; bondage is distraction by senses, liberation is their control.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya directly controls the senses by redirecting prana.

teachings.teaching: Therefore, controlling the six enemies (mind, senses, etc.), he should wander with devotion to Krishna, detached from petty pleasures.
teachings.lens_note: The six enemies are overcome through Kriya's systematic purification.

teachings.teaching: He may enter villages, towns, etc., for alms, and wander the earth with holy places and forests.
teachings.lens_note: Pilgrimage is external; the real journey is inward through Kriya.

teachings.teaching: By constantly begging in the vānaprastha āśramas, one quickly attains perfection, free from delusion and with pure sattva.
teachings.lens_note: Perfection comes from unwavering focus on the self, as in Kriya.

teachings.teaching: He should not see the visible world as real, for it perishes; detached, he should cease from desires for this world and the next.
teachings.lens_none: This is the discriminative wisdom cultivated in Kriya.

teachings.teaching: The world, consisting of mind, speech, and prana, is all māyā; by reasoning, the self-established one should forget it.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya practice reveals the illusory nature of the world.

teachings.teaching: One who is established in knowledge, detached, or a devotee, may abandon the external marks of āśrama and wander beyond rules.
teachings.lens_note: Beyond external forms, the true sannyāsī is inwardly absorbed in Krishna, as in Kriya.

teachings.teaching: The wise should play like a child, act like a fool, speak like a madman, and wander like a cow, following the Vedic path.
teachings.lens_note: This paradoxical behavior reflects the freedom of one who has transcended social norms through inner realization.

teachings.teaching: He should not be attached to Vedic debates, nor be a heretic or logician; he should not take sides in dry arguments.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma warns against intellectualism without practice; Kriya is experiential.

teachings.teaching: He should not be disturbed by people nor disturb them; he should tolerate insults and not insult anyone.
teachings.lens_note: Equanimity in all situations is a sign of Kriya mastery.

teachings.teaching: The self is one in all beings, like the moon reflected in water; all beings are of one essence.
teachings.lens_note: This non-dual vision is the goal of Kriya.

teachings.teaching: If he does not get food, he should not despair; if he gets it, he should not rejoice; both are under divine control.
teachings.lens_note: Acceptance of God's will is central to Kriya.

teachings.teaching: He should strive only for food enough to sustain life, for by that he can contemplate truth and attain liberation.
teachings.lens_note: The body is maintained only for spiritual practice, as in Kriya.

teachings.teaching: He should eat whatever food comes by chance, whether good or bad, and similarly accept clothing and bedding.
teachings.lens_note: Non-preference is a form of surrender.

teachings.teaching: The jñānī should perform purification, sipping, bathing, and other duties not by scriptural injunction but as a pastime, like Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Actions become spontaneous and playful when one is established in the self.

teachings.teaching: For him, duality is destroyed by the vision of Krishna; even if it appears until death, he ultimately attains Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: The Kriya practitioner gradually overcomes duality through constant remembrance.

teachings.teaching: When one becomes detached from pleasures that end in misery, he should approach a guru to learn about Krishna's dharma.
teachings.lens_note: The guru is essential in Kriya for initiation and guidance.

teachings.teaching: The devotee should serve the guru with faith and without envy until he realizes Brahman, which is Krishna himself.
teachings.lens_note: Service to guru is a form of Kriya, purifying the heart.

teachings.teaching: One who has not controlled the six senses, with strong senses as charioteer, devoid of knowledge and detachment, but lives by the triple staff, is a hypocrite.
teachings.lens_note: External renunciation without inner control is useless; Kriya provides the inner discipline.

teachings.teaching: Such a person denies the gods, the self, and Krishna, and falls from both this world and the next.
teachings.lens_note: Hypocrisy leads to spiritual downfall.

teachings.teaching: The dharma of a bhikṣu is peace and non-violence; of a forest-dweller, austerity and insight; of a householder, protection of beings and sacrifice; of a brāhmaṇa, serving the guru.
teachings.lens_note: Each stage has its own discipline, but all culminate in devotion.

teachings.teaching: Celibacy, austerity, purity, contentment, and friendliness to all beings are common to all, as is worship of Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: These universal virtues are the foundation of Kriya.

teachings.teaching: One who worships Krishna constantly through his own dharma, with single-minded devotion, attains firm devotion and the feeling of Krishna in all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Devotion is the essence of Kriya, leading to non-dual realization.

teachings.teaching: Through unswerving devotion, he attains Krishna, the Lord of all worlds, the cause of creation and dissolution.
teachings.lens_note: Devotion is the highest path, integrating all Kriya practices.

teachings.teaching: Thus, with heart purified by his own dharma, knowing Krishna's path, endowed with knowledge and realization, he soon attains Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: The culmination of Kriya is direct realization of Krishna.

teachings.teaching: This dharma of the varṇāśrama system is characterized by conduct; when combined with devotion to Krishna, it is the supreme means to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that all dharmas are means to devotion, which is the ultimate Kriya.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna defines true knowledge, devotion, and the path to liberation. He explains that jñāna (knowledge) is seeing the one reality behind all manifestations, and vijñāna (realization) is perceiving the threefold transformations of matter. He then describes bhakti-yoga as the supreme means, listing its practices. Uddhava asks for definitions of various virtues and concepts, and Krishna provides them, culminating in the teaching that the highest good is to transcend both good and bad qualities.

story.title: The Definition of True Knowledge and Devotion
story.arc: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain the path of devotion and the meaning of various spiritual terms; Krishna responds with a comprehensive discourse.
story.timeline_note: After the Mahabharata war, Yudhiṣṭhira had asked Bhīṣma about dharma; now Krishna recounts that teaching to Uddhava.
story.comic_potential: A dialogue between a divine teacher and a devoted disciple, with a list of definitions that could be presented as a glossary.

teachings.teaching: True knowledge is seeing the one reality behind all manifestations, and realizing the threefold transformations of matter.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the discernment between Purusha and Prakriti, and the recognition of the three gunas as the basis of all change.

teachings.teaching: Bhakti-yoga is the supreme means to attain Me, consisting of hearing, chanting, worship, service, and offering everything to Me.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that bhakti-yoga integrates all aspects of Kriya-Yoga, where devotion is the driving force behind meditative practices.

teachings.teaching: The highest good is to transcend both good and bad qualities, realizing the self as distinct from the gunas.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of going beyond the pairs of opposites through inner purification and meditation.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna explains the three paths of yoga (jñāna, karma, bhakti) suited to different types of aspirants, emphasizing that bhakti-yoga is the most direct and effective means to attain liberation. He describes the process of detachment, control of mind, and the ultimate supremacy of devotion over all other practices.

story.title: The Three Paths of Yoga
story.arc: Uddhava questions the purpose of Vedic injunctions; Krishna responds with the three yogas.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita discourse before Krishna's departure.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful yet profound teaching style contrasts with Uddhava's earnest questioning.

teachings.teaching: The three yogas (jñāna, karma, bhakti) are prescribed for different types of aspirants: jñāna-yoga for the detached, karma-yoga for the active, and bhakti-yoga for those with faith but not fully detached or attached.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that bhakti-yoga integrates action and knowledge through devotion, making it accessible to all.

teachings.teaching: One should perform prescribed duties without desire for results until detachment arises; then one can pursue jñāna or bhakti.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on disciplined action as a foundation for higher practices.

teachings.teaching: The human body is a rare boat to cross the ocean of material existence; one who does not utilize it is self-destructive.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights the urgency of using the human birth for spiritual practice, a core tenet of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Control of the mind through yoga, including pranayama and meditation, is essential; the mind can be subdued by constant practice and detachment.
teachings.lens_note: This directly corresponds to Kriya-Yoga's techniques of breath control and meditation to still the mind.

teachings.teaching: Bhakti-yoga is the supreme path; even if one commits a sin, it can be burned by yoga, and devotion alone can destroy all karmas and grant liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees bhakti as the culmination, where devotion purifies the heart and leads to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: A pure devotee does not desire even liberation; they are indifferent to all material rewards and solely devoted to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of nishkama bhakti (desireless devotion) as the highest state.

chapter_summary: The Lord explains the nature of dharma and adharma, purity and impurity, and the purpose of Vedic injunctions. He describes how attachment to sense objects leads to bondage, and how the Vedas ultimately point to Him. He criticizes those who are attached to the fruits of rituals and do not understand the inner meaning of the scriptures.

story.title: The Lord's Discourse on Dharma and the Vedas
story.arc: Teaching
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gītā, after Kṛṣṇa's departure from Dvārakā.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily philosophical discourse.

teachings.teaching: One's dedication to one's own duty is a virtue; deviation is a fault.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of svadharma as a foundation for spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Purity and impurity are relative; they are determined by context and purpose.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as a call to discernment in practice, not rigid externalism.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas prescribe duties according to time, place, and person to guide karma.
teachings.lens_note: Aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on disciplined action as a means to inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Attachment to sense objects leads to desire, anger, delusion, and loss of self-awareness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights this as the core obstacle in Kriya-Yoga: the need to withdraw senses from objects.

teachings.teaching: The promise of heavenly rewards in the Vedas is meant to attract the ignorant, not as ultimate good.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is a reminder that the goal is liberation, not temporary pleasures.

teachings.teaching: Those attached to rituals and their fruits do not know the Lord within.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would say this underscores the need for inner realization beyond external rites.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas ultimately describe the Lord; their essence is to lead to Him.
teachings.lens_note: This is the culmination: all practices in Kriya-Yoga are meant to realize the Self as the Lord.

chapter_summary: Uddhava asks Krishna about the number of tattvas (elements) enumerated by various sages. Krishna explains that the differences arise from the interplay of his maya, and that the true knowledge is beyond such enumeration. He describes the evolution of prakriti, purusha, and the guṇas, and explains how the jiva transmigrates due to karma and ignorance.

story.title: The Enumeration of Tattvas
story.arc: Uddhava's inquiry about the number of elements and Krishna's explanation of the diversity of philosophical views as arising from maya.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita discourse before Krishna's departure.
story.comic_potential: A philosophical dialogue with no dramatic action; could be visualized as a serene teaching scene.

teachings.teaching: The different enumerations of tattvas by sages are due to the influence of my maya; all are valid from their perspective.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that intellectual debates about categories are secondary to direct experience of the Self through practice.

teachings.teaching: The jiva transmigrates due to the mind's attachment to sense objects, and the cycle continues until one realizes the Self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight that Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind and senses, enabling the practitioner to break the cycle of rebirth.

teachings.teaching: The body undergoes nine stages (conception, birth, childhood, etc.) but the Self is unchanging.
teachings.lens_note: This supports the Kriya-Yoga goal of realizing the immutable Self beyond bodily changes.

teachings.teaching: The distinction between prakriti and purusha is a product of maya; in reality, they are not separate.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga aims to transcend duality through meditation, realizing the oneness of the individual self with the Supreme.

chapter_summary: In Bhagavata Purana 11.22, Krishna explains to Uddhava the nature of the self and the world, using analogies like a tree and its seed, a dancer and spectator, and moving water and trees. He describes how the ignorant soul, deluded by the three gunas, cycles through different births. Uddhava then asks how even the wise can be overcome by prakriti, except those devoted to Krishna.

story.title: Krishna's Discourse on the Self and Prakriti
story.arc: Uddhava seeks understanding of how the wise are overcome by nature
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita section of Bhagavata Purana
story.comic_potential: Analogies of tree, dancer, and moving water could be visually depicted

teachings.teaching: The self is distinct from the body, just as a seer of a tree is distinct from the tree and its seed and fruit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the witness consciousness (sakshi) that is separate from the fluctuations of prakriti.

teachings.teaching: The ignorant soul, not discriminating the self from prakriti, becomes deluded by sense contact and enters samsara.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of discerning the purusha from prakriti through meditation.

teachings.teaching: Through association with sattva, one becomes a sage or deity; with rajas, an asura or human; with tamas, an animal or plant.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the result of karmic tendencies that can be transformed by Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Even though the self is inactive, it appears to act due to the mind's identification with the gunas, like a spectator imitating a dancer.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of non-doership (akartritva) realized in deep meditation.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a dream; even though objects are unreal, the cycle of samsara continues as long as one dwells on them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind to see the dream-like nature of reality.

teachings.teaching: One should not enjoy sense objects with the false senses, but see the illusory nature of the world.
teachings.lens_note: This is a call to withdraw the senses (pratyahara) in Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Even when insulted or harmed by others, one should lift oneself by the self, seeking the highest good.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as cultivating inner strength through Kriya-Yoga, unaffected by external circumstances.

teachings.teaching: Prakriti is very powerful, even over the wise, except for those devoted to Krishna's dharma.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the need for divine grace and surrender, which in Kriya-Yoga is attained through devotion and meditation.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna narrates to Uddhava the story of a greedy brahmin from Avanti who lost his wealth and family, became disillusioned, and took up the life of a mendicant. Despite being abused by wicked people, he remained steadfast in his practice and sang a song of wisdom about the mind being the root cause of bondage and liberation.

story.title: The Song of the Avanti Brahmin
story.arc: A wealthy but miserly brahmin loses everything due to his own faults, becomes a wandering monk, endures abuse, and attains wisdom through detachment.
story.timeline_note: Narrated by Krishna to Uddhava as a past event.
story.comic_potential: High contrast between the brahmin's former opulence and his later life as a beggar, with absurd insults from villagers.

teachings.teaching: Wealth is the root of many evils; one should abandon the pursuit of wealth for spiritual progress.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle of aparigraha (non-possessiveness) as a foundation for inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: The mind is the sole cause of bondage and liberation; controlling the mind is the highest yoga.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga directly purifies the mind, making it steady for samadhi.

teachings.teaching: One should not blame others for one's suffering; the mind is the real enemy.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that external circumstances are neutral; the mind's reaction creates suffering.

teachings.teaching: Even a little greed destroys one's good qualities, like a spot of leprosy ruins beauty.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees greed as a vritti (mental modification) that must be dissolved through pratyahara.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (11.23.050-060) presents a philosophical analysis of the causes of happiness and suffering. It systematically refutes external causes such as deities, the body, planets, karma, and time, concluding that suffering arises from the mind's delusion (ātma-vibhrama). The solution is to control the mind through devotion to Mukunda (Krishna), as taught by a sage who remained unshaken despite adversity.

story.title: The Sage's Song on Transcending Dualities
story.arc: Philosophical discourse on the nature of suffering and the path to liberation through devotion.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Bhagavata Purana's teachings on yoga and detachment.
story.comic_potential: A sage, having lost all possessions, wanders and is mistreated by lowly people, yet remains unshaken, teaching a profound lesson on inner peace.

teachings.teaching: Suffering is not caused by external factors like deities, the body, planets, karma, or time; it arises from the mind's delusion (ātma-vibhrama).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle that the mind's identification with the body and external events creates suffering; true freedom comes from realizing the Self as distinct.

teachings.teaching: One should control the mind with intelligence fixed on the Lord; this is the essence of yoga.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga involves directing the mind inward through disciplined practice, ultimately merging it with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and reciting this song of the sage protects one from being overcome by dualities.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the power of śravaṇa (hearing) and kīrtana (chanting) in Kriya-Yoga as means to stabilize the mind.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna explains the Sāṅkhya philosophy of creation and dissolution, describing the evolution from pure consciousness through prakṛti, guṇas, mahat, ahaṅkāra, tan-mātras, indriyas, devatās, and the cosmic egg, culminating in the manifestation of the three worlds and the process of involution back to the supreme self.

story.title: Sāṅkhya Yoga: The Philosophy of Creation and Dissolution
story.arc: Krishna expounds the Sāṅkhya system to Uddhava, detailing the evolution from pure consciousness to the material world and the reverse process of involution back to the self.
story.timeline_note: This teaching occurs in the context of Krishna's final instructions to Uddhava before his departure from the world.
story.comic_potential: The abstract philosophical discourse could be visualized as a cosmic diagram showing the hierarchy of tattvas and the cycle of creation and dissolution.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate reality is one without duality; the world is a manifestation of māyā.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the non-dual Self through the cessation of mental modifications.

teachings.teaching: Prakṛti and Puruṣa are the two fundamental principles; from their interaction arises the universe.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the practical discrimination (viveka) between the seer and the seen as the core of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The process of creation and dissolution is cyclical, governed by time and the guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view of the breath cycle as a microcosm of cosmic creation and dissolution.

teachings.teaching: By understanding the Sāṅkhya philosophy, one cuts the knot of doubt and attains liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that intellectual understanding must be complemented by direct yogic experience (kriya) to dissolve ignorance.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna explains to Uddhava the three modes of material nature (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their manifestations in human qualities, actions, and states of consciousness. He describes how to transcend the modes through bhakti-yoga and attain liberation.

story.title: The Modes of Nature and Transcendence
story.arc: Krishna's discourse on guṇas and bhakti-yoga
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita section of the Bhagavata Purana.
story.comic_potential: A philosophical dialogue with no dramatic action.

teachings.teaching: The three modes (sattva, rajas, tamas) determine one's qualities and destiny. Sattva leads to happiness and knowledge, rajas to action and attachment, tamas to ignorance and suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the modes are not merely theoretical but are experienced in daily life. Sattva corresponds to clarity and inner peace, rajas to restless activity, tamas to inertia. Through disciplined practice (kriya), one can observe and transcend these modes.

teachings.teaching: The mixed state (sannipāta) is the sense of 'I' and 'mine', which binds the soul.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets 'I' and 'mine' as the ego-identification with body and mind. Kriya-Yoga aims to dissolve this by realizing the self as distinct from the guṇas.

teachings.teaching: One can infer a person's predominant guṇa by their qualities: sattva through calmness, rajas through desire, tamas through anger.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework uses self-observation to detect which guṇa is active. For example, anger indicates tamas; desire indicates rajas; peace indicates sattva.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna without desire for results is sattvic; devotion with desire for rewards is rajasic; devotion with violence is tamasic.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses that true bhakti is nirguṇa (transcendental) when performed without any expectation, aligning with the Kriya-Yoga ideal of action without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The guṇas are born from the mind (citta) and bind the soul. They belong to prakṛti, not to the self or to God.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the mind is the field where guṇas operate. By stilling the mind through meditation, one transcends them.

teachings.teaching: When sattva predominates, one experiences happiness, dharma, and knowledge. When rajas predominates, one experiences suffering, action, and fame. When tamas predominates, one experiences delusion, sleep, and violence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma correlates these states with the three states of consciousness: waking (sattva), dreaming (rajas), deep sleep (tamas). The fourth state (turīya) is beyond guṇas.

teachings.teaching: Signs of sattva: peaceful mind, sense control, fearlessness, detachment. Signs of rajas: restlessness, mental agitation, bodily discomfort. Signs of tamas: dullness, inability to grasp, loss of mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma uses these as diagnostic criteria for self-assessment in meditation practice.

teachings.teaching: Sattva increases the strength of gods, rajas of demons, tamas of ghosts. Sattva leads to wakefulness, rajas to dreaming, tamas to sleep. The fourth state (turīya) is beyond all three.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets gods, demons, and ghosts as psychological forces within: sattva elevates, rajas agitates, tamas degrades. Turīya is the state of pure consciousness.

teachings.teaching: By sattva one goes upward, by rajas stays in the middle, by tamas goes downward. Those who die in sattva go to heaven, in rajas to the human world, in tamas to hell. The nirguṇa attain Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the goal is not just to reach heaven but to transcend all guṇas and attain liberation, which is the ultimate aim of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Action offered to Krishna without desire is sattvic; action with desire for results is rajasic; action full of violence is tamasic. Knowledge that leads to oneness is sattvic; knowledge that sees diversity is rajasic; materialistic knowledge is tamasic; knowledge fixed on Krishna is nirguṇa.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that all actions, knowledge, and even living situations can be transformed by dedicating them to the divine.

teachings.teaching: Living in the forest is sattvic, in a village rajasic, in a gambling den tamasic; living in Krishna's abode is nirguṇa. A detached worker is sattvic, a passionate worker rajasic, a confused worker tamasic; one who takes shelter of Krishna is nirguṇa.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets 'forest' as solitude for meditation, 'village' as social engagement, 'gambling den' as addiction. The nirguṇa state is constant remembrance of Krishna.

teachings.teaching: Faith in spiritual life is sattvic, faith in karma is rajasic, faith in adharma is tamasic; faith in serving Krishna is nirguṇa. Food that is healthy and pure is sattvic, food that pleases the senses is rajasic, food that causes pain is tamasic.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma applies this to diet and lifestyle choices in Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Happiness from the self is sattvic, from sense objects is rajasic, from delusion and misery is tamasic; happiness in Krishna is nirguṇa. All factors—object, place, result, time, knowledge, action, doer, faith, state, form, and goal—are influenced by the three guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees every aspect of life as colored by guṇas; the practice is to gradually shift from tamas to rajas to sattva to nirguṇa.

teachings.teaching: All phenomena are made of guṇas, supported by the puruṣa and the unmanifest. Everything seen, heard, or contemplated is within the guṇas. The cycles of rebirth are due to guṇas and karma. One who conquers these guṇas through bhakti-yoga attains Krishna's nature.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that bhakti-yoga, especially Kriya-Yoga, is the direct means to transcend the guṇas and realize one's true identity.

teachings.teaching: Having obtained this human body, which is the basis for knowledge and realization, one should give up association with the guṇas and worship Krishna. A wise person, detached and self-controlled, should conquer rajas and tamas by cultivating sattva, and then conquer sattva by complete indifference, becoming free from guṇas and attaining Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga provides systematic techniques (kriyas) to purify the mind, cultivate sattva, and ultimately transcend even sattva to realize the self as Brahman, identical with Krishna.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna teaches Uddhava about detachment from sense objects and the importance of association with saints, using the story of King Pururava (Aila) who became infatuated with the celestial nymph Urvashi. Pururava laments his bondage to lust and realizes the futility of material pursuits. Krishna concludes that by giving up bad association and taking shelter of saintly persons, one can attain devotion and liberation.

story.title: The Story of King Pururava and Urvashi
story.arc: Krishna narrates the story of King Pururava who became madly attached to the celestial nymph Urvashi, lost his kingdom and dignity, and eventually realized the futility of material attachment. He then renounced and attained liberation.
story.timeline_note: The story is set in ancient times, before the Mahabharata war.
story.comic_potential: The scene of Pururava running naked after Urvashi, crying and begging her to stay, has strong visual and emotional contrast.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with materialistic people who are addicted to sense gratification, as such association leads to downfall.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle of avoiding vikshepa (distraction) and cultivating sattvic associations to stabilize the mind in meditation.

teachings.teaching: The body is impure and temporary; attachment to it and to sense objects is based on ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the body is a tool for sadhana, not an end. Recognizing its impurity helps in detaching from sensory pleasures.

teachings.teaching: Association with saintly persons (sadhus) cuts the knots of material attachment and leads to devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, satsanga is crucial for purifying the mind and awakening the inner guru.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and chanting the glories of the Lord with faith leads to bhakti.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's path includes japa and kirtan as means to focus the mind and invoke divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate refuge for those drowning in the ocean of material existence.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of surrendering to the divine will and realizing one's true nature.

chapter_summary: Uddhava asks Krishna to explain the process of worship (kriyā-yoga) for devotees, including those of lower castes. Krishna describes the three types of sacrifice (Vedic, Tantric, mixed), the preparation for worship, the types of images, the steps of pūjā (bathing, decorating, offering), fire sacrifice, meditation on the Lord's form, and the benefits of establishing temples and gardens.

story.title: Kriyā-yoga: The Path of Worship
story.arc: Uddhava inquires about the practical method of worshiping the Lord, and Krishna responds with a detailed exposition of the process, including the types of sacrifices, preparation, image worship, fire sacrifice, and the rewards of establishing temples.
story.timeline_note: This is part of the Uddhava Gītā, a discourse given by Krishna to Uddhava before Krishna's departure from the world.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a technical manual on worship procedures.

teachings.teaching: The three types of sacrifice: Vedic, Tantric, and mixed. One should worship the Lord according to one's chosen path with faith and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the method must be aligned with one's nature and capacity, yet all paths lead to the same goal when performed with devotion.

teachings.teaching: Worship can be performed on various substrates: an image, a diagram, fire, the sun, water, or in the heart. The key is devotion and sincerity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses that the external form is secondary; the internal attitude of surrender and love is paramount.

teachings.teaching: The eight types of images: stone, wood, metal, clay, painted, sand, mental, and jewel. They are either movable or fixed.
teachings.lens_note: This classification shows that worship can be adapted to available resources; the essence is the mental focus on the Lord.

teachings.teaching: Even a little water offered with devotion is more pleasing to the Lord than abundant offerings from a non-devotee.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga highlights that quality of devotion (bhakti) outweighs quantity of material. The inner state is what matters.

teachings.teaching: The process of worship includes purification, meditation on the Lord's form, offering various items, and concluding with prayers and surrender.
teachings.lens_note: This structured practice is a form of Kriya-Yoga that purifies the mind and senses, leading to inner stillness and connection with the Divine.

teachings.teaching: By worshiping the Lord through Vedic and Tantric methods, one attains both material and spiritual success.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the integration of outer ritual and inner meditation, leading to harmony in life and liberation.

teachings.teaching: Establishing temples and gardens for the Lord's service leads to great spiritual rewards, including attaining the Lord's abode or even equality with Him.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching encourages community service and creating sacred spaces, which in Kriya-Yoga are seen as extensions of one's own spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana discusses the results of worship, including attaining Brahma-loka or equality with the Lord through three types of worship, and emphasizes that only through detached bhakti-yoga one attains the Lord. It also warns against stealing the livelihood of brahmanas and describes the karmic shares of various participants in actions.

story.title: Results of Worship and Bhakti-Yoga
story.arc: Instructional discourse on the outcomes of different types of worship and the importance of detached devotion.
story.timeline_note: Part of a larger dialogue between Lord Krishna and Uddhava.
story.comic_potential: Low; primarily doctrinal instruction.

teachings.teaching: Through worship with offerings, one attains Brahma-loka; through three types of worship, one attains equality with the Lord; but only through detached bhakti-yoga one attains the Lord directly.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that external rituals (puja) yield limited results, while inner, detached devotion (bhakti-yoga) leads to union with the divine. The 'three types' may correspond to tamasic, rajasic, and sattvic worship, but only nishkama bhakti is ultimate.

teachings.teaching: One who steals the livelihood of a brahmana (or a god) becomes a worm-eater for millions of years.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the karmic law of cause and effect; in Kriya-Yoga, it warns against misusing one's position or resources, as it creates severe bondage.

teachings.teaching: The doer, the charioteer, the instigator, and the approver all share in the fruits of actions, proportionally.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights collective karma: every participant in an action bears responsibility. This encourages mindfulness of one's associations and intentions.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, Lord Krishna instructs Uddhava on the nature of the self and the world, emphasizing non-attachment to dualities, the illusory nature of material existence, and the path to liberation through knowledge, devotion, and yoga. Uddhava questions the basis of samsara, and Krishna explains that it arises from identification with the body and mind, which can be overcome by discerning the true self as pure consciousness. He describes the means to cut the root of samsara, including jnana, viveka, and bhakti-yoga, and addresses obstacles in yoga practice.

story.title: The Yoga of Discrimination and Devotion
story.arc: Uddhava questions the nature of samsara; Krishna explains the illusory nature of duality and the path to liberation.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Uddhava Gita, before Krishna's departure from the world.
story.comic_potential: A dialogue between a divine teacher and a devoted disciple, with abstract concepts that could be visualized through metaphors like the sun dispelling darkness.

teachings.teaching: One should neither praise nor criticize the nature and actions of others, seeing the whole world as one with the self through nature and the person.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the practice of non-judgment and equanimity, essential for stilling the mind.

teachings.teaching: The self alone is the entire universe; it creates, sustains, and destroys all. There is no other reality apart from the self.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of realizing the unity of the individual self with the cosmic self.

teachings.teaching: Samsara (worldly existence) arises from the contact of the self with the body, senses, and prana, and is fruitful only for the ignorant.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that samsara is a product of ignorance; through Kriya-Yoga, one can transcend this contact.

teachings.teaching: The root of samsara is the mind's identification with the body and senses; it can be cut by the sword of knowledge sharpened by devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga practice of using meditation and devotion to sever attachments.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon attachment to the objects of maya through firm bhakti-yoga until the impurities of the mind are destroyed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga integrates bhakti as a means to purify the mind and attain self-realization.

teachings.teaching: Obstacles in yoga can be overcome by various means: asanas, tapas, mantras, herbs, meditation on the Lord, chanting his names, or following the yoga masters.
teachings.lens_note: This provides a practical framework for Kriya-Yoga practitioners to address challenges on the path.

teachings.teaching: The body is impermanent; therefore, one should not be overly attached to it but continue yoga practice with devotion to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma advises that the body is a tool for spiritual practice, not an end in itself.

chapter_summary: Uddhava asks Krishna about the difficulty of yoga practice and the path to liberation. Krishna responds by teaching the essence of bhakti-yoga, emphasizing devotion, equality, and surrender. He instructs Uddhava to go to Badarikashrama and practice detachment. Uddhava, overwhelmed with love, departs for the forest, attaining liberation.

story.title: Uddhava's Liberation and the Essence of Bhakti-Yoga
story.arc: Uddhava questions Krishna about yoga; Krishna teaches the path of devotion; Uddhava departs for Badarikashrama and attains liberation.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's discourse on yoga in previous chapters, Uddhava seeks further clarification.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's emotional departure, with tears and repeated prostrations, has dramatic and poignant potential.

teachings.teaching: Perform all actions for Krishna, gradually remembering Him, with mind and heart fixed on Him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the essence of kriya-yoga—offering every action to the divine, aligning thought and deed.

teachings.teaching: See Krishna in all beings, both externally and internally, and see the self in the self, like the sky in a clear mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the practice of samadhi, realizing the omnipresence of the divine.

teachings.teaching: Honor all beings with the thought of Krishna, taking shelter of knowledge alone.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the cultivation of equal vision, a key aspect of kriya-yoga.

teachings.teaching: See equally a brahmana, a dog-cooker, a thief, a cow, an elephant, a dog, and a dog-eater.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the pinnacle of non-dual perception, transcending social distinctions.

teachings.teaching: By constantly cultivating the thought of Krishna in all beings, envy, contempt, and pride quickly vanish.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the purification of the mind through constant practice.

teachings.teaching: Prostrate like a staff before all beings, even a dog, until the sense of Krishna's presence arises in all.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the practice of humility and surrender, essential for kriya-yoga.

teachings.teaching: When one realizes all as Brahman, one ceases all activities, freed from doubt.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the culmination of knowledge, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The path of devotion to Krishna in all beings is the best of all paths; it never fails.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the assurance of success in kriya-yoga.

teachings.teaching: One who hears this teaching with faith and devotion is not bound by karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the fruit of listening and practicing.

teachings.teaching: This knowledge should not be given to the hypocritical, atheist, or dishonest.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This emphasizes the need for a qualified disciple.

teachings.teaching: After knowing this, nothing remains to be known; it is like drinking nectar.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the completeness of the teaching.

teachings.teaching: When a mortal renounces all actions and surrenders to Krishna, he attains immortality and becomes one with Him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the final goal of kriya-yoga—union with the divine.

chapter_summary: Krishna orchestrates the destruction of the Yadu dynasty through a curse, leading to a fratricidal war at Prabhasa. After the Yadava annihilation, Balarama departs the world, and Krishna is accidentally shot by a hunter named Jara. Krishna then instructs his charioteer Daruka to inform the survivors and departs from the world, while his chariot ascends to heaven.

story.title: The Destruction of the Yadu Dynasty and Krishna's Departure
story.arc: Climax of the Yadu saga; Krishna's earthly pastime concludes.
story.timeline_note: After the Mahabharata war, Krishna's divine plan to relieve the earth's burden leads to the Yadava destruction.
story.comic_potential: The fratricidal battle at Prabhasa, with warriors fighting with eraka reeds that turn into iron clubs, has a surreal, tragicomic quality.

teachings.teaching: The Yadava destruction illustrates the inevitable consequences of divine will and the transient nature of worldly power.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this event symbolizes the dissolution of ego and attachment through the force of divine grace, leading to the realization of the self as distinct from the body and mind.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's instruction to Daruka to remain detached and focused on dharma and jnana highlights the path of renunciation and inner peace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true yoga involves inner detachment (upekṣaka) and steady knowledge (jñāna-niṣṭha), as taught to Daruka.

teachings.teaching: The hunter Jara's accidental killing of Krishna and subsequent forgiveness demonstrates the power of surrender and the Lord's compassion.
teachings.lens_note: This episode illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle that even unintentional actions can lead to liberation when offered to the divine with humility.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the departure of Krishna from the mortal world, his return to his own abode through yogic absorption, the reactions of various beings, the subsequent grief of the Vrishnis, the submergence of Dvārakā, and Arjuna's establishment of Vajra as king. It concludes with the phalaśruti of hearing or reciting these pastimes.

story.title: Krishna's Departure and the End of the Yadu Dynasty
story.arc: Krishna concludes his earthly pastimes, returns to his abode, and the Yadus perish. Dvārakā is submerged, and Arjuna establishes Vajra as king.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the Yadu fratricidal war described in the previous chapter.
story.comic_potential: The simultaneous arrival of all celestial beings, Krishna's yogic departure, the dramatic grief of the Vrishnis, and the submergence of Dvārakā provide vivid visual scenes.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's actions, including his birth and death, are illusory displays (māyā-viḍambanam) like an actor's performance.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that the divine play is a teaching tool to inspire detachment and inner realization; the external events are symbolic of inner processes.

teachings.teaching: One who recites or hears Krishna's pastimes with faith is freed from all sins and attains supreme devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight that such recitation is a form of kriya-yoga, purifying the mind and leading to direct experience of the divine.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's body is not subject to material laws; he left it by yogic fire and entered his own abode, demonstrating the power of yoga.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle of conscious exit from the body through mastery of prana and agni.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (12th Canto, Chapter 1) is a prophecy by Śuka to King Parīkṣit about the future dynasties that will rule in the Kali Yuga, starting from the end of the Mahabharata war. It lists kings of the Śiśunāga, Nanda, Maurya, Śuṅga, Kāṇva, Āndhra, and other dynasties, culminating in a period of mleccha (foreign) rulers who are adharmic and cause suffering. The chapter emphasizes the decline of dharma and the eventual end of the Kali Yuga.

story.title: Prophecy of Future Kings in Kali Yuga
story.arc: Śuka narrates the dynasties that will rule after the Mahabharata war, from the Śiśunāgas to the mlecchas, ending with the decline of dharma.
story.timeline_note: Set after the Mahabharata war, during the reign of Parīkṣit, foretelling events from the beginning of Kali Yuga up to its end.
story.comic_potential: A montage of kings and dynasties rising and falling, with visual contrasts between righteous and adharmic rulers, culminating in chaotic mleccha rule.

teachings.teaching: The Kali Yuga is characterized by adharma, with kings becoming increasingly corrupt and foreign, leading to suffering.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this decline reflects the loss of inner discipline (kriya) and alignment with dharma. The prophecy warns that without self-cultivation, society degenerates.

teachings.teaching: The cycle of dynasties shows the impermanence of power and the inevitable decay of material civilization.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a reminder that only the eternal Self (ātman) is permanent; attachment to transient rulership leads to suffering. Kriya-Yoga practice detaches one from such impermanence.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the degradation of dharma in the Kali Yuga, the eventual appearance of Kalki avatar to restore righteousness, and the cyclical nature of the four yugas. It also lists the dynasties of kings and the duration of Kali Yuga.

story.title: The Degradation of Kali Yuga and the Advent of Kalki
story.arc: Prophetic description of the Kali Yuga's moral decline, the appearance of Kalki to restore dharma, and the cyclical return of Krita Yuga.
story.timeline_note: Set in the future relative to the Mahabharata, describing the Kali Yuga and its end.
story.comic_potential: High contrast between the degenerate Kali Yuga and the divine intervention of Kalki, with vivid imagery of social collapse and restoration.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, wealth becomes the sole criterion for birth, conduct, and qualities; dharma and justice are based on power.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that external markers (wealth, power) are not the true measure of spiritual progress; inner purification through kriya is essential.

teachings.teaching: Marriage is based on mutual inclination, business on deceit, and social status is determined by external symbols like the sacred thread.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would note that such superficiality arises when inner spiritual practice is neglected; true relationships and dharma require inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: People will abandon their homes and take refuge in forests, subsisting on roots and fruits, due to oppression by greedy rulers.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the external manifestation of inner turmoil; Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that one must cultivate inner stability to withstand external chaos.

teachings.teaching: The maximum human lifespan in Kali Yuga will be 30 to 20 years due to the effects of the age.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma might interpret this as a consequence of diminished prana and spiritual vitality; Kriya-Yoga aims to extend life and consciousness through pranayama.

teachings.teaching: When dharma is lost and the world is overrun by imposters, the Lord will incarnate as Kalki to destroy the wicked and restore righteousness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens sees this as the cosmic response to collective spiritual bankruptcy; individual kriya practice can preempt such decline by aligning with divine will.

teachings.teaching: After Kalki's advent, people's minds will become pure, and they will be devoted to Vasudeva, leading to a sattvic population and the return of Krita Yuga.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would emphasize that this purification is the collective result of individual inner purification; Kriya-Yoga is the direct path to such purity.

teachings.teaching: The cycle of four yugas (Krita, Treta, Dvapara, Kali) repeats endlessly.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as a macrocosmic reflection of the microcosmic cycles of breath and consciousness; mastery of kriya allows one to transcend time.

teachings.teaching: Kings and rulers who become attached to the earth ultimately perish, leaving only their names in stories.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would caution against identifying with the temporary body and possessions; true identity is the eternal Self, realized through kriya.

chapter_summary: The Earth laments the folly of kings who fight for her, forgetting death. Śuka describes the decline of dharma through the four yugas, culminating in Kali Yuga's degradation. He extols the power of remembering Kṛṣṇa, especially at death, as the sole remedy for Kali's defects.

story.title: The Lament of the Earth and the Glories of Kṛṣṇa in Kali-yuga
story.arc: Śuka describes the futility of worldly conquest, the decline of dharma through the yugas, and the supreme efficacy of remembering Kṛṣṇa.
story.timeline_note: Set after the Mahābhārata war, during the onset of Kali-yuga.
story.comic_potential: The Earth personified laughing at kings; vivid descriptions of Kali-yuga degradation; dramatic contrast between past glories and present decay.

teachings.teaching: Kings who fight for the Earth are like playthings of death; their conquests are futile.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this highlights the need to turn inward: external victories are temporary, while inner conquest of the senses is the true battle.

teachings.teaching: The four yugas are characterized by the predominance of sattva, rajas, and tamas, and the gradual decline of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees the yugas as internal states: Kṛta-yuga corresponds to sattvic purity, Tretā to rajasic action, Dvāpara to mixed rajas-tamas, and Kali to tamasic ignorance. The teaching urges practitioners to cultivate sattva through Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: In Kali-yuga, people are degraded: greedy, cruel, and devoid of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that these external conditions mirror internal impurities. Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind, enabling one to transcend the age's negativity.

teachings.teaching: The only remedy for Kali's defects is constant remembrance and chanting of Kṛṣṇa's names.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga incorporates nāma-japa as a powerful technique to still the mind and connect with the divine, leading to liberation.

chapter_summary: This verse contrasts the primary spiritual practices in each of the four yugas: meditation on Vishnu in Krita Yuga, performing sacrifices in Treta Yuga, worship in Dvapara Yuga, and chanting Hari's name in Kali Yuga. It emphasizes that in Kali Yuga, the same result as in previous yugas is achieved through Hari-kirtana.

story.title: The Power of Hari-kirtana in Kali Yuga
story.arc: A single verse comparing spiritual practices across yugas, culminating in the efficacy of chanting Hari's name in Kali Yuga.
story.timeline_note: Refers to the four yugas: Krita, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali.
story.comic_potential: Minimal; a didactic verse without narrative.

teachings.teaching: In Kali Yuga, the same spiritual result as in previous yugas is achieved through chanting the name of Hari (kirtana).

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the four types of cosmic dissolution (pralaya): nitya (constant), naimittika (occasional), prākṛtika (elemental), and ātyantika (ultimate). It details the process of elemental dissolution during prākṛtika pralaya, explains the illusory nature of the universe through analogies, and concludes with the glory of hearing the Bhagavata Purana and its transmission lineage.

story.title: The Four Dissolutions and the Glory of the Bhāgavata
story.arc: Cosmic dissolution and the ultimate reality
story.timeline_note: The chapter describes cyclical cosmic time scales and the ultimate dissolution beyond time.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic sequence of elemental dissolution (fire, wind, water) and the final state of pure consciousness could be visualized as a cosmic implosion.

teachings.teaching: The universe undergoes four types of dissolution: nitya (constant change), naimittika (at the end of Brahmā's day), prākṛtika (elemental dissolution at the end of Brahmā's life), and ātyantika (final liberation).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these dissolutions correspond to progressive states of meditation: nitya is the constant dissolution of thoughts, naimittika is the temporary cessation of mental modifications, prākṛtika is the dissolution of the subtle body, and ātyantika is the final realization of the Self.

teachings.teaching: The material elements dissolve sequentially: earth into water, water into fire, fire into air, air into ether, ether into ego, ego into mahat, mahat into unmanifest prakṛti, and prakṛti into time.
teachings.lens_note: This sequence mirrors the inward journey in Kriya-Yoga, where the yogi withdraws the senses and elements into their source, culminating in the realization of pure consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate reality (Brahman) is beyond all states (waking, dream, deep sleep) and is described as 'like a sleeping person, like emptiness, inconceivable'.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that this state is the goal of Kriya-Yoga: turīya, the fourth state beyond the three ordinary states, which is pure awareness.

teachings.teaching: The world is an illusion (māyā) superimposed on the Self, like clouds appearing in the sky. The Self alone is real.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner learns to discriminate between the transient and the eternal, realizing that all phenomena are appearances in consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate dissolution (ātyantika pralaya) is the cutting of the knot of ego (ahaṅkāra) through discrimination, leading to direct experience of the Self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma identifies this with the final stage of Kriya-Yoga where the yogi, through sustained practice, destroys the ego and abides in the Self.

teachings.teaching: For one desiring to cross the ocean of saṃsāra, there is no boat other than the constant immersion in the nectarean stories of the Lord's pastimes.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets 'stories' as the inner experience of the divine through Kriya-Yoga meditation, which is the only means to liberation.

chapter_summary: Śuka instructs King Parīkṣit on the nature of the self, distinguishing it from the body and mind, and teaches that the self is eternal, unborn, and identical with Brahman. He advises the king to give up the animalistic notion of death and realize his true nature as the supreme self, beyond the gunas and the cycle of birth and death.

story.title: Instruction on the Eternal Self
story.arc: Śuka teaches Parīkṣit the nature of the self and the means to transcend death.
story.timeline_note: This is the final teaching to Parīkṣit before his death by snakebite.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic contrast between the king's impending death and the teaching of immortality.

teachings.teaching: The self is unborn, eternal, and not subject to death. The body is like a pot; when broken, the space within merges with outer space. Similarly, when the body dies, the jīva merges with Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the self as distinct from the body-mind complex, achieved through meditative absorption (samādhi) where one directly experiences the self as pure consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The mind creates bodies, qualities, and actions. Māyā creates the mind, leading to the cycle of saṃsāra. The self is the witness, like the space in a pot, unaffected by the pot's breaking.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the mind is the instrument of bondage; through Kriya-Yoga, one stills the mind and transcends its creations, realizing the self as the unchanging witness.

teachings.teaching: The self is self-luminous, beyond the manifest and unmanifest, and is the support like space. It is eternal, infinite, and incomparable.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of realizing the self as the supreme light, beyond all dualities, through the practice of pratyāhāra and dhāraṇā.

teachings.teaching: One should contemplate the self within oneself, using the intellect imbued with discrimination and devotion to Vāsudeva. Then even Takṣaka's bite will not harm, for one has realized the self as the death of death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga, as a form of devotion and meditation, leads to the direct realization of the self as the Lord, making one immune to the fear of death.

teachings.teaching: The realization 'I am Brahman, the supreme abode' leads to liberation. By seeing the self as distinct from the body and the universe, one transcends death.
teachings.lens_note: This is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga: the identity of the individual self with the universal self, achieved through sustained practice and grace.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit, having heard the Purāṇa from Śuka, attains liberation and is bitten by Takṣaka. His son Janamejaya performs a snake sacrifice to avenge him, but is persuaded to stop by Bṛhaspati. The chapter then describes the origin of the Vedas from Oṃkāra and their division by Vyāsa.

story.title: The Liberation of Parīkṣit and the Origin of the Vedas
story.arc: Parīkṣit attains liberation after hearing the Purāṇa; his death by snakebite leads to Janamejaya's snake sacrifice, which is halted by Bṛhaspati's wisdom. The narrative then shifts to the cosmic origin of sound and the Vedas.
story.timeline_note: Events occur after the Mahābhārata war; Parīkṣit's death and Janamejaya's sacrifice are in the Kali Yuga. The Vedic origin section is timeless.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Parīkṣit's serene death, Takṣaka's deceit, Janamejaya's wrath, Indra's intervention, and Bṛhaspati's wise counsel. The cosmic sound creation offers visual spectacle.

teachings.teaching: One should not fear death when absorbed in the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of pratyahara and dharana, where the mind is fixed on the inner Self, transcending bodily fears.

teachings.teaching: Life and death are determined by one's own karma; no external agent gives happiness or suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes self-effort and the law of karma; this teaching reinforces personal responsibility and the futility of blaming others.

teachings.teaching: One should tolerate insults, not disrespect anyone, and not create enmity based on the body.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the yama of ahimsa and the practice of equanimity in Kriya-Yoga, essential for inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate goal is the supreme abode of Viṣṇu, attained by those free from the sense of 'I' and 'mine' regarding body and home.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the state of kaivalya, realized through deep meditation and detachment from material identification.

teachings.teaching: The primal sound Oṃkāra is the seed of all Vedas and the manifestation of the Supreme Brahman.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga uses Oṃkāra as a primary mantra for pranayama and meditation, leading to the realization of the inner Self.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the transmission of the Vedas from Vyasa to his disciples and the subsequent branching of Vedic recensions. It details how Paila, Vaisampayana, Jaimini, and Sumantu received the Rig, Yajur, Sama, and Atharva Vedas respectively, and how their disciples further subdivided the texts. The story of Yajnavalkya's conflict with his guru Vaisampayana and his receiving the Vajasaneyi Yajus from the Sun god is also narrated.

story.title: The Transmission of the Vedas and the Story of Yajnavalkya
story.arc: This chapter traces the lineage of Vedic teachers from Vyasa through his disciples and their successors, culminating in the story of Yajnavalkya who, after a dispute with his guru Vaisampayana, receives the Vajasaneyi Yajus from the Sun god.
story.timeline_note: The events occur after the Mahabharata war, during the Dvapara Yuga transition to Kali Yuga.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic conflict between Yajnavalkya and Vaisampayana, and Yajnavalkya's intense penance to the Sun god, offer vivid scenes.

teachings.teaching: The Vedas are transmitted through a lineage of teachers and disciples, emphasizing the importance of guru-parampara.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the necessity of receiving knowledge through a living tradition and personal initiation.

teachings.teaching: Yajnavalkya's prayer to the Sun god describes the Sun as the inner controller and the one who awakens the soul from ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets the Sun as the inner Self (Atman) that illumines the mind and senses, and the practice of meditation on the Sun as a means to realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: The Sun god grants Yajnavalkya the Vajasaneyi Yajus, symbolizing that divine grace can bestow knowledge when one is rejected by human teachers.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle that sincere practice (kriya) can attract divine intervention, a key theme in Sharma's Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the lineage of teachers of the Atharva Veda and the Puranas, enumerating the eighteen major Puranas and their characteristics according to the ten lakṣaṇas (characteristics) of a Purana, as taught by Sūta to the sages.

story.title: The Lineage of Vedic and Puranic Teachers and the Characteristics of Puranas
story.arc: Sūta narrates the paramparā of Atharva Veda and Purana teachers, then defines the ten lakṣaṇas of a Purana, lists the eighteen major Puranas, and concludes with the glory of this knowledge.
story.timeline_note: The chapter discusses the transmission of knowledge from ancient times, referencing the lineage from Vyāsa and his disciples.
story.comic_potential: A visual depiction of the teacher-student lineage as a branching tree, with each sage holding a scroll, and the ten lakṣaṇas illustrated as icons.

teachings.teaching: A Purana is defined by ten characteristics: sarga (creation), visarga (secondary creation), vṛtti (maintenance), rakṣā (protection), antarāṇi (intermediate periods), vaṃśa (dynasties), vaṃśānucarita (dynastic chronicles), saṃsthā (dissolution), hetu (cause), and apāśraya (ultimate refuge).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these ten characteristics correspond to the ten stages of yogic practice: from the initial creation of intention (sarga) to the final absorption in the Self (apāśraya).
